fr:mainmatter.html:p: More formal notes that follow a textbook / curriculum.

Concept-level notes on C++, working from the ground up: the kinds of types you can define, how concrete and abstract types differ in their memory model, the machinery behind runtime polymorphism, and the language features that govern immutability, type deduction, error handling, and storage. Each section transcludes an atomic tree; the notebook is the organizing spine, so links to "C++" anywhere in the forest resolve here.

C++ gives several ways to bundle data into a named type. A class is the workhorse — it separates interface from implementation through access control, and carries the constructor/destructor machinery that ties resource lifetime to scope. A struct is the same mechanism with public defaults, a union overlays members in shared storage, and an enumeration names a fixed set of values.
Need an abstraction to separate implementation from interface. This is done in C++ via the public and private members of a class.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A struct is class with its members public by default.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A constructor is a function of a class that shares the same name as the class.
A constructor without parameters is called a default constructor.
A destructor is the mechanism in C++ that ensures that any memory a constructor allocates is deallocated.
A destructor is a function of a class that shares the same name as the class with the ~ operator to denote it is the complement of the constructor.

A struct is a class whose members are public by default. The two are the same mechanism — struct simply flips the default access — so the choice is a convention: use struct for passive aggregates of data with no invariant to maintain, and class when access control is enforcing an invariant.
A union is a struct in which all members are allocated at the same address. This ensures that the union occupies only as much space as its largest member.
A union can hold a value for only one member at a time.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: No automatic lifetime management for non-trivial types. If a union holds an object with a non-trivial constructor/destructor (like std::string), the compiler won't call them for you. You must manually placement-new and explicitly call the destructor, and getting this wrong causes leaks or corruption. union U { std::string s; int i; }; // destructor not called automatically

fr:mainmatter.html:p: User-defined type in C++ that is a set of named values designed to make code more readable and less error prone.

fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
enum class Color { red, green, blue };
Color c = Color::red;

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If you want enumerator values to be int, you can use enum.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Use enumerations to represent sets of named constants
Prefer enum class to enum when possible

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The pivotal design axis is whether a type's representation is part of its definition. A concrete class commits to its layout, so it can live on the stack and be copied by value; an abstract type hides representation entirely behind pure virtual functions, which forces heap allocation and pointer-based use. The container is the running example that both build on.
A class that behaves "just like built-in types". The defining characteristic of a concrete type is that its representation is part of its definition.
Because the representation is fixed you can

html:ol.html:li: Allocate objects directly on the stack.
Copy and assign them by value
Have them as direct members of other classes.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: complex, vector, and string are concrete classes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A container is a concrete class that exists to hold elements..

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: We call vector a container.
We can initialize a container via std::initializer_list, where you directly pass the list of elements via {1,2,3,4,...}. Alternatively, containers may support additional methods like push_back().

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An abstract type is a type that completely insulates a user from implementation details.
Since we do not know anything about the representation (in particular, the size) we must allocate everything on heap.

Abstract types are only useful because of dynamic dispatch. A virtual function call is resolved through a hidden per-object pointer into a per-class virtual function table, which also carries the type information that makes dynamic casting possible.
Each class with a virtual function gets a virtual table: a static, per-class, array of function pointers with one entry per virtual function.
The virtual table is created by the compiler and is stored in the object's memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: vtable layout: [ offset-to-top ] // slot -2 [ type_info* ] // slot -1 ← RTTI [ &virtual_fn_0 ] // slot 0 [ &virtual_fn_1 ] // slot 1 ...

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The type_info pointer is needed to support runtime type-checking / dynamic casting .

fr:mainmatter.html:p: During construction, the vptr is initialized to point to the base class's vtable. In each classes vtable, there is a thunk (offset-to-top) that tells

fr:mainmatter.html:pre: offset 0: [ vptr_A ] ← D's primary vtable (A subobject) offset 8: [ a ] offset 16: [ vptr_B ] ← D's secondary vtable (B subobject) offset 24: [ b ] offset 32: [ d ]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every object of a class with a virtual function carries a hidden vptr (virtual pointer) that points to the class's virtual table.

Three keywords that shift guarantees and work between the programmer, the reader, and the compiler. const is a promise not to mutate, constexpr pushes that promise further into compile-time evaluation, and auto hands type deduction to the compiler — indispensable for generic programming.
A hint to the compiler in C++, meaning "I promise not to change this value".

A hint to the compiler in C++ that the value or function can be evaluated at compile time. This allows the compiler to pre-compute the function / value and hardcode it into the resulting binary, eliminating any instructions involved in the computation from the final binary.

html:p.html:em: Idea: Shift work from every run on every user's machine to once on the build machine.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A constexpr function is not forced to run at compile time. It is only done only when the arguments are const.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: auto is automatic type deduction in C++.
In Generic Programming, auto is crucial because types may not be known.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: When should you NOT use auto?

In C++ they provide two reasons:
When you want to make the type visible clearly to the readers of your code.
You want to be explicit on the variable range or precision i.e. auto x = 1; will be an int. If you want to enforce a double, then you must specify so explicitly.
auto x{v}; deduces single value's type in modern C++, but in C++11/14 it deduces std::initializer_list. auto x = {v}; remains an std::initializer_list in modern C++.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In C++, exceptions are thrown via the throw keyword.
The throw keyword transfers control to exception handler. This then proceeds as

html:ol.html:li: Allocate heap memory for the exception object.
Invoke the unwinder.
Walk the stack frame-by-frame to checking to see if any frame has a catch with matching type.
Walks the stack again, restoring registers / SP per frame.

html:ul.html:li: Cleaning up all the invalid stack frames between the throw and catch.

html:ol.html:li: Transfers control to the catch block.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If no matching catch block is found, the program calls std::terminate
You can annotate a function with noexcept to indicate that it never throws exceptions. This is mainly used as an annotation to enable better choices by the compiler

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The compiler may call copy constructor instead of move constructor if the move constructer is not marked noexcept; If an exception were thrown during moving, then the object would be in a broken state.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In C++, you allocate memory on the heap with new and delete.
Notes on the book Inside the Machine by Jon Stokes, restructured as a concept hierarchy.

The CPU's nearest tier of storage — the first NUMA level. Registers sit physically next to the ALUs and are addressed by name in the instruction encoding rather than by memory address. Their count is limited by the chip's surface area, which is why main memory exists at all.

The register file is the structure that holds the architecturally-visible registers. To support a superscalar machine, the file needs enough read and write ports to feed every ALU simultaneously: for an n-ALU chip with two operands per op, that's 2n read ports and n write ports.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Die area for a register file scales quadratically in the number of ports.
Split it. Different execution units (integer, floating-point, vector) get their own smaller register files. Fewer ports per file, faster access, less area.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Beyond the architecturally-named registers, modern chips keep many extra rename registers used by Register renaming to break false data dependencies.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Instructions distinguish "immediate types" from "register types" so the decoder knows whether a field encodes a register name or a literal value.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The classical model of CPU operation: each instruction goes through three phases.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch: read the instruction from memory into the instruction register; increment the program counter.
Decode: extract the opcode, the operand register names, and the destination from the instruction register.
Execute: route to the appropriate functional unit — ALU for arithmetic, load/store unit for memory ops, branch unit for branches.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Instructions don't take arbitrary time. The motherboard's clock cycle bounds when latched values become visible to the next stage.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Pipelined execution decomposes this loop into independently-running pipeline stages so multiple instructions are in flight at once. Superscalar machines run multiple instructions per stage. Both are extensions of this same three-phase model.
An instruction that potentially changes the program counter to something other than "the next sequential instruction." Two flavors:

html:ol.html:li: Unconditional: the PC is set to the destination address, period.
Conditional: the PC is set to the destination only if some condition holds.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Conditional branches require the hardware to remember the outcome of recent arithmetic — typically in a processor status word (PSW) register, with one bit per condition (zero, sign, carry, overflow). Each conditional-branch variant tests a particular PSW bit.

In a pipelined CPU, branches stall the front-end: until the branch resolves, the fetcher doesn't know what to fetch next. This is the Control hazard, and modern machines mitigate it with Speculative execution driven by Branch prediction.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If the program uses relative addressing, the OS supplies a code-segment base to add to the branch destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The fixed series of steps a machine performs from power-on until a usable operating system is running. Each stage runs from a known location and hands off control to the next.

html:ol.html:li: Power signal resets the CPU to a known state.
The CPU fetches from a hard-wired address — the BIOS (or UEFI firmware). The BIOS runs basic hardware checks (POST).
The BIOS loads and jumps to the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from a known location on disk.
The bootloader places the kernel image at the correct address and jumps to its entry point.
The kernel performs early setup: installs the Trap table (a special privileged instruction), starts the timer interrupt, sets up memory management, and finally enters user space.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The hardware is hard-wired only to find the BIOS. Everything past that is software the user can replace, which is why dual-booting and custom bootloaders work.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Decomposing the Fetch-execute loop into independently-running stages so that multiple instructions are in flight simultaneously. A classical four-stage pipeline:

html:ol.html:li: Fetch from memory.
Decode.
Execute (the original execute phase, minus the write).
Write the result back to register or memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each stage runs concurrently on different instructions. The clock cycle is bounded by the slowest stage, not the whole loop — so dividing the work into roughly-equal-length stages is essential. With n stages running in steady state, the machine retires one instruction per cycle even though each instruction takes n cycles to traverse.

html:ol.html:li: Completion rate: instructions completed per nanosecond. Program time is \frac {\text {instructions per program}}{\text {avg completion rate}}.
Instruction throughput: instructions retired per clock cycle.
Instruction latency: cycles to complete one instruction (\geq the number of stages, often more in practice).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Pipelining doesn't make any one instruction faster — it raises throughput. Pushing this further (deeper pipelines, more stages) shrinks the clock cycle but increases per-instruction latency and the cost of pipeline stalls or flushes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: What stops the steady-state throughput of one instruction per cycle is hazards: Data hazard, Structural hazard, and Control hazard. The next layer of complication is going superscalar — multiple instructions per stage — which is covered in Superscalar architecture.

Multiple Execution unitss on the same chip running in parallel during the same pipeline stage. Decode becomes decode/dispatch: the front-end must decide which instructions can issue in parallel and route them to the right units. Going superscalar amplifies the cost of every pipeline Data hazard and Control hazard, which is why the rest of this chapter's machinery — Forwarding, Register renaming, Branch prediction — exists.
The functional circuitry that actually performs an instruction. A superscalar CPU contains several, dispatched to in parallel during the execute stage of the Pipelined execution. The common families:

html:ul.html:li: ALU — arithmetic logic unit, performs arithmetic (add, sub, mul, div) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations on integers and on special registers like the PSW. Typically split into integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
Load-store unit — performs memory loads and stores. Carries a small address-calculation adder for address-mode resolution.
Branch execution unit — evaluates branch conditions (often by reading the PSW) and signals whether the branch is taken.
Vector / SIMD units — operate on wide registers (SSE, AVX, AltiVec) for data-parallel work.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Per the convention in Inside the Machine, "ALU" often refers to any execution unit for arithmetic or logical operations — including integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each unit's reads and writes flow through a Register file. Splitting the register file per-unit reduces port count and access time.

The internal organization of a CPU that implements a given instruction set architecture (ISA). Two chips can present identical ISAs to software while having radically different microarchitectures — e.g. Intel and AMD both implement x86, but their pipelines, branch predictors, and execution units differ.

The bridge between the two is the microcode engine: a mini-CPU inside the CPU. It holds small programs in a microcode ROM and runs them when a complex instruction needs to be translated into the simpler primitives the hardware actually executes. Conceptually, it's an emulator.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need large microcode engines to implement their complex instructions. RISC designs minimize the microcode engine, pushing translation to the compiler instead.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The translated primitives are Micro-operations — a finer-grained, more uniform instruction stream that the actual hardware schedules. The translation step is the main reason an x86 chip's pipeline starts with such complex decode logic.

When one instruction's result is the next instruction's input, a naive Pipelined execution would have to stall: the second instruction can't execute until the first has written back. Data hazards are the most pervasive source of stalls in a CPU.
Two standard mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Forwarding — route the producer's output directly into the consumer's input, before the writeback stage commits it. The consumer sees the result one cycle earlier.

Register renaming — false dependencies, where two instructions write to the same architectural register but operate on independent values, can be broken by mapping the architectural register to two different physical registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Data hazard mitigation: the producing instruction's result is routed from the output of the execute stage directly to the input of the next instruction's execute stage, bypassing the register-file write and read. The dependent consumer can execute one cycle later instead of waiting for writeback.
Conceptually, the pipeline grows wiring rather than stages. The result is "forwarded" from where it appears to where it's needed.

A Data hazard mitigation: dynamically remap architectural register names to a larger pool of physical registers. False data dependencies — where two instructions write to the same architectural register but use independent values — get broken because the hardware can map each write to a different physical register.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Consider: add r1, r2, r3 // r2 := r1 + r3 add r4, r5, r2 // r2 := r4 + r5 (reuses r2 for an independent value) The second add only depends on r2 as a destination, not as a source. Without renaming, it would have to wait for the first instruction's writeback to avoid clobbering. With renaming, the second write goes to a fresh physical register, and the two instructions can proceed in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The programming model exposes only the architecturally-named registers; the Register file hides a larger pool of rename registers underneath. This is one of the central tricks that makes out-of-order execution viable.

Two simultaneous instructions need the same hardware resource, and there's only one of it. The classic example is a single-port Register file facing two writes in one cycle, or two memory-accessing instructions facing a single load-store unit.
Mitigations cost area:

html:ul.html:li: Duplicate the resource (extra ALU, more register-file write ports).
Pipeline the resource (multi-cycle dividers that accept a new input each cycle).
Stall — accept the cost in throughput.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Register file area scales quadratically in port count, so duplicating ports for many ALUs gets expensive quickly.
Per-unit register files (one for integer, one for FP, one for vector) with fewer ports each. Discussed under Register file.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When the CPU reaches a Branch instruction in the Pipelined execution, it doesn't yet know what to fetch next. Until the branch's condition resolves and its target is computed — several stages later — fetch is stuck.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Branch prediction — guess where control flow is going.
Speculative execution — actually run instructions down the predicted path, ready to throw the work away if the guess was wrong.
Instruction caching — keep recently-fetched instructions handy so the cost of restarting fetch on a mispredict is at least bounded by cache latency, not DRAM latency.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The cost of a mispredicted branch is roughly the pipeline depth — every speculatively-issued instruction has to be squashed and the pipeline refilled. Deeper pipelines amplify this cost, which is one reason Pentium 4 vs G4e: design philosophies (deep pipeline, narrow & deep) and Superscalar architecture-style designs (wider, shallower) make different tradeoffs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism that lets the front-end of a pipelined CPU keep fetching past a Branch instruction before the branch has actually resolved. The fetcher consults a predictor; if the prediction is right, the Pipelined execution runs at full throughput; if wrong, the speculatively-issued instructions are squashed.

Program-agnostic rules: e.g. "always predict taken", "always predict not-taken", or "predict backward branches taken, forward branches not-taken". Cheap, but accuracy is poor outside of loop-heavy code.
Driven by per-branch history kept in two hardware tables:

html:ul.html:li: Branch history table (BHT) — indexed by branch address, holds a small counter (typically 2-bit saturating) tracking how often the branch was taken.
Branch target buffer (BTB) — holds the target address each branch was observed to jump to. A BTB miss is expensive: even if you correctly predict "taken", you still don't know where to fetch from.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Per-branch prediction allows a counter to learn pattern-specific behavior — e.g. a loop counter takes the back-edge N - 1 times then falls through.

The Pentium famously combined the two: static prediction on a BHT miss, dynamic prediction otherwise. Later machines added refinements — PowerPC 750 added a Trace cache-like branch target instruction cache (BTIC) that stores the first few instructions at the target, eliminating the re-fetch latency. The Pentium M added a loop detector for loops that always iterate the same number of times.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: See Speculative execution for what happens once a prediction has been made.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Run instructions before knowing whether they should actually be run, on the assumption that you'll usually be right. Used after Branch prediction to keep the pipeline busy past a Branch instruction: instructions on the predicted path enter the pipeline, execute, and stash their results in non-architectural state. Only when the branch resolves are speculated results "committed" to architectural state — or, if the prediction was wrong, discarded.
Speculative state lives in the Reorder buffer (and the Register renaming rename registers). Entries are tagged as speculative; on misprediction the speculative entries are flushed.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Modern speculative execution drives a lot of CPU complexity — and a lot of CPU security work. Spectre-class side channels exploit the fact that speculative execution can leave traces in caches even after the speculative results are discarded.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Execute instructions in whatever order their operands become available, then commit them back in program order. The programmer's model still sees sequential execution; the hardware reorders aggressively underneath to keep Execution units busy across Data hazards and memory latency.
Two structures make this work:

html:ul.html:li: Reservation stations — hold instructions waiting on operands, so a stalled instruction doesn't block independent ones behind it.
Reorder buffer — preserves program order so commits look sequential and mispredicted Speculative execution paths can be unwound.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Combined with Register renaming to break false dependencies, the back-end of a modern CPU is essentially a dynamic scheduler that uses the program order only as a hint about what to retire next.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: An out-of-order pipeline can issue a load early (because its address operands are ready), execute several unrelated ALU ops while waiting for the memory, then have all four instructions ready to commit in program order. The illusion of sequential execution is preserved; the parallelism is real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The structure that lets an Out-of-order execution CPU look in-order to the programmer. The ROB holds every in-flight instruction in original program order, regardless of the order they actually executed. When the oldest entry has its result ready and all earlier entries have already committed, the ROB commits it — writing its result to architectural state and freeing the slot.

For stores, commit is only half the story. The ROB entry is freed and the instruction is architecturally real, but the value has not yet reached the cache — it is handed off to the Store buffer for asynchronous drain. This is what keeps the ROB from stalling behind every cache miss: once a store is known to be non-speculative, the front-end can move on.
Each ROB entry typically tracks:

html:ul.html:li: Instruction identity and program order position.
Operand register names (and renamed sources).
Status — pending, executing, complete, speculated.
The instruction's result, held in a data field that doubles as a Register renaming register.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Speculatively-issued instructions also enter the ROB but are tagged. On a branch misprediction, all speculative entries past the mispredicted branch are squashed.

The Pentium Pro introduced this design with a 40-entry ROB. The ROB sits at both ends of the pipeline — dispatch enters it, retirement leaves it — which is what makes the rest of the back-end free to issue and execute out of order.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: ROB size, Reservation station count, and pipeline depth set each other's bounds. A shallow pipeline can afford a small ROB; a deep one (Pentium 4 had 126) needs a large one to keep the back-end fed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A buffer in front of an Execution units (or group of them) that holds dispatched instructions waiting on their operands. Once all operands arrive — either by writeback or by Forwarding — the instruction is issued to its execution unit. Reservation stations exist so that an instruction waiting on a slow operand doesn't block dispatch of unrelated instructions behind it.
Two organizational choices:

html:ul.html:li: One reservation station per execution unit (Pentium Pro). Smaller, simpler.
One shared station feeding multiple units (also seen on Pentium Pro, where it issues up to five micro-ops per cycle).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a single station, instructions issue in FIFO order. Across stations, there is no guarantee — that's what out-of-order execution actually means.
Dispatching an instruction into a reservation station has four prerequisites:

html:ol.html:li: In-order rule: instructions dispatch in program order (they execute out of order, not dispatch).
Execution unit availability: the matching reservation station has room.
Completion buffer availability: the Reorder buffer has room to track the new in-flight instruction.
Register-rename availability: enough rename registers exist to hold the new instruction's destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism by which a modern out-of-order CPU notices that a previously-stalled instruction now has all its operands ready. The issue queue holds the N in-flight instructions whose operands are not yet available; each entry watches a small set of physical register tags (the operands it is waiting for). When a producer finishes, it broadcasts "tag T is ready" to every entry, and each entry compares that tag against its own waiting tags through a content-addressable memory (CAM). Entries whose last operand arrives become eligible to issue this cycle; a separate select stage picks which of the now-ready entries actually fires.

The cost is the reason CPU issue queues are small. Each entry runs a CAM lookup against each broadcast tag, so an N-entry queue with N producers per cycle scales as O(N^2) in comparators, wires, and power. The select stage compounds this — priority encoders across N entries are themselves a quadratic-area structure on the critical timing path. The cumulative wakeup-select latency is what bounds clock frequency once N grows past a few tens.

This is the structural reason CPU out-of-order windows stay in the tens-to-low-hundreds of entries even as transistor budgets grow. Wider doesn't help: timing closure on the CAM degrades faster than the additional ILP repays. GPUs sidestep the whole problem by leaning on Thread-level parallelism — a cheap per-register scoreboard plus a warp scheduler covers stalls without any broadcast/compare structure.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Wakeup-select sits at the boundary between Reservation station (which holds the instruction) and Register renaming (which named the tags being broadcast). All three structures together are what people mean by "the out-of-order back-end."

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two design philosophies for an instruction set architecture.

html:ul.html:li: Complex instruction set computing (CISC) — many specialized instructions, including ones that combine memory access with arithmetic or operate on complex types (strings, decimal numbers). One instruction often does the work of several. x86 is the canonical example.

Reduced instruction set computing (RISC) — a small set of simple, uniform instructions; explicit load and store; arithmetic only on registers. Each instruction does little but maps cleanly to hardware. PowerPC, MIPS, ARM are RISC.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: CISC needs a fat Microarchitecture engine to translate its complex instructions into hardware-executable primitives. That's silicon area, power, and design complexity that doesn't directly add throughput.

Once RISC clearly won as a hardware model, x86 vendors adopted instruction translation: a CISC front-end decodes x86 into RISC-like Micro-operations internally. The chip is RISC on the inside, CISC on the outside.

The cost of legacy is real. The original Pentium spent roughly 30% of its transistors on legacy support (mostly the microcode ROM). The Pentium Pro spent 40% on backward compatibility. Modern x86 chips have absorbed this overhead because they can — transistor budgets grew faster than the legacy tax.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware-internal instruction stream that an x86 chip actually schedules. Each user-visible x86 instruction is decoded into one or more micro-ops, which are simpler, more uniform, RISC-like primitives that the back-end can execute, reorder, and rename freely.

Many common x86 instructions translate to just one or two micro-ops, so decoders are split into simple and complex variants — the simple decoders are specific to single-micro-op instructions and run in parallel with the complex one.
Optimizations that operate on the micro-op stream:

html:ul.html:li: Micro-op fusion (Pentium M) — two related micro-ops that the back-end tracks together while sharing a slot in the issue queue. A store decomposes into a store-address and store-data pair, which are fused; a read-modify decomposes into a load + ALU op that fuse and execute serially.

Macro fusion (Core 2 Duo) — fuse a pair of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode, so a single micro-op represents both. Increases peak decode bandwidth without growing ROB or RS pressure.
Trace cache (Pentium 4) — cache decoded micro-op sequences for hot code so decoding doesn't have to happen on every iteration.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Micro-ops exist because CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need to be translated to something the hardware can schedule cleanly. Pure RISC chips don't need them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An L1-level cache of decoded Micro-operations sequences. Instead of caching raw x86 instructions that have to be decoded on every fetch, a trace cache stores micro-op traces ready for the back-end. Introduced in the Pentium 4 to keep its very deep pipeline fed when normal x86 decoding couldn't run fast enough.
The trace cache operates in two modes:

html:ul.html:li: Execution mode: on a trace hit, feed the cached micro-ops directly to the back-end. Decode is bypassed entirely.
Build mode: on a trace miss, fetch the instructions from L2, decode them, and assemble a new trace while feeding it forward.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The trace cache is indexed during Branch prediction, so speculative paths beyond a branch can be stored in the same trace and fed forward without restarting fetch. This lets a single cache line hold six micro-ops that may include a speculated branch target.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Long x86 instructions that decode to hundreds of micro-ops would blow out the trace cache.
Tag those instructions. When the trace cache encounters a tag, it stores a pointer to the microcode ROM rather than the micro-ops themselves, and the ROM streams them on demand.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU clock is decreasing much faster than main-memory latency, so without a cache hierarchy a fast CPU spends most of its time stalled on memory.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If you are a really fast factory, the size of the truck doesn't matter — you must still wait the full duration of the first delivery.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple tiers of progressively-larger, progressively-slower storage between the CPU's registers and main memory. Each tier holds the most-recently-used contents of the next slower tier, exploiting Spatial locality and Temporal locality in the workload.
Approximate tiers on a modern x86 chip:

html:ul.html:li: Registers — ns, dozens of bytes per core.
L1 cache — 2–8 ns, 8–128 KB per core. Often split into instruction and data L1s.
Built from static RAM (4–6 transistors per cell), which is fast but area-expensive vs. DRAM's single-transistor cell.
L2 cache — 5–12 ns, 0.5–8 MB. Closes the latency gap between L1 and DRAM.
L3 cache — slower, shared across cores.
Main memory — 10–60 ns, GBs.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A byte's journey. L1 checked first; on miss, L2; on miss, main memory; on miss in main memory, Page fault to disk.

L1 is split into instruction and data caches because the locality profiles of code and data are very different — re-running the same code over different data has high temporal locality for code but low for data.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A cache miss happens when the requested data isn't in cache and has to be fetched from a slower tier. Misses fall into four categories — the "four Cs" of cache misses:

html:ol.html:li: Compulsory miss — the data was never in cache. Cold-start misses are unavoidable for a given cache size and working set.
Conflict miss — the data was evicted because of an associativity collision. A different access mapped to the same set kicked it out. Reduced by higher Cache associativity.
Coherence miss — the data was invalidated because another core wrote to it. Specific to shared-memory multicore systems.
Capacity miss — the cache filled up and the data was evicted by Cache replacement policy. The working set doesn't fit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Distinguishing them matters because they have different mitigations: conflict misses respond to associativity changes, capacity misses respond to size, coherence misses respond to cache-coherence protocol design, and compulsory misses respond to prefetching.

The empirical observation that if a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access addresses near x soon. Sequential array traversal, struct field access, and instruction fetch all exhibit it.

Caches exploit spatial locality by loading whole cache lines (typically 64 bytes) on every miss, not just the single requested byte. The byte that triggered the load is the critical word; the surrounding bytes come along for free under the assumption you'll want them.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: RAM and caches are organized in cache-line granularity throughout. Bandwidth is sized for whole-line transfers; tags are per-line, not per-byte.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The complement is Temporal locality — repeated access to the same data over time. Temporal locality implies spatial locality (you re-touch nearby bytes when you re-touch the original), but not vice versa.
If a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access x again soon. Loop variables, frequently-called functions, and re-used data structures all exhibit it.

Caches exploit temporal locality by retaining recently-accessed lines until something more important takes their slot. The Cache replacement policy is what decides "more important" — typically LRU and the clock algorithm-ish.
Temporal locality implies spatial locality (re-touching a byte tends to re-touch its neighbors) but not vice versa: a sequential scan has spatial locality with no temporal locality.
The rule that decides which slots in the cache an address is allowed to occupy. Three points on the spectrum:

html:ul.html:li: Direct mapped — every address maps to exactly one slot. Fast (only one tag to check) but suffers conflict misses: two hot addresses that map to the same slot evict each other.
Fully associative — any address can live in any slot. No conflict misses but tag check is expensive (compare against every entry in parallel).

n-way set associative — middle ground. The cache is divided into sets of n slots; an address maps to a specific set but can occupy any of its n slots. The hot setting on real caches is somewhere between 4-way and 16-way.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Higher associativity reduces conflict misses but increases tag-check hardware and power. The cache tag of an address is the bits that aren't used to pick the set — they're stored alongside the data and compared on every access.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The tag array is stored separately from the data array (tag RAM) so the tag comparison can happen in parallel with the data lookup. Tags are small, allowing many in-flight comparisons.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a Cache associativity set, when a new line arrives and the set is full, which existing line gets evicted? Same question as LRU and the clock algorithm for pages, different timescales.

The hot policy is least recently used — but pure LRU requires tracking a per-line ordering and updating it on every hit, which is too expensive at L1 latencies. Real caches use pseudo-LRU — a tree of single bits per set that approximates LRU with a fraction of the bookkeeping. The clock algorithm is one well-known pseudo-LRU scheme.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Block (cache line) size is part of the same tradeoff. Larger blocks improve Spatial locality but cause cache pollution (you fetch bytes you don't use) and eat memory bandwidth. The sweet spot is around 64 bytes on modern x86.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a cached line is modified, when do you propagate the change to the next tier?

html:ul.html:li: Write-through — every store updates both the cache and main memory. Simple, but generates memory traffic on every write.

Write-back — stores update only the cache. The line is marked dirty via a per-line bit; the next-tier copy is updated only when the line is evicted. Less memory traffic but the cache is now the sole authoritative source until eviction, which complicates correctness.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Real CPUs run write-back at L1 and L2. Write-through is occasionally used for specific lines (e.g. memory-mapped device registers where every write must hit the device immediately).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-back is one of the constraints behind cache-coherence protocols on multicore chips: every core must agree on which one currently owns a dirty line.
The hard limit on per-core clock speed that emerged in the early 2000s, ending the era of frequency scaling. Power dissipation has two components on a chip:

html:ul.html:li: Dynamic power density — watts per unit area dissipated by transistor switching. Scales with clock frequency: faster switching, more energy per second. You can also drive it up by cramming more transistors into the same area.
Static power density — watts per unit area dissipated by leakage current through "off" transistors. Smaller transistors leak more, so denser chips leak more even when idle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By the mid-2000s, single-core frequency scaling was thermally unsustainable. The industry pivoted to low-power design and Multicore — get more throughput from many slower cores instead of one faster one. Pentium M and the Core series were the first generations explicitly designed under this constraint.

Multiple independent CPU cores on the same chip. A core, for this purpose, is the bundle of front-end, back-end, Execution unitss, and L1 Cache hierarchy that runs one architectural thread of execution.

Traditional multiprocessor systems put each core on its own physical chip; chip multiprocessing (CMP) puts multiple cores on the same die. The key advantage is shared lower-level caches — typically an L2 or L3 shared across all cores on the chip — which speeds up inter-core communication and amortizes silicon.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The shift to multicore was forced by the Power wall more than chosen for its own merits. The software cost is real: shared-memory parallelism requires correctness work that single-threaded code didn't.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multicore is what makes Concurrency a hardware problem, not just a scheduling problem: real simultaneous execution on different physical cores means software has to handle Race conditions with real synchronization primitives like Locks.

What the CPU does, entirely in hardware, the moment an interrupt arrives — whether a device IRQ, a fault like a Page fault, or a software int N. The point of the hardware path is to land in the kernel at a clean instruction boundary with enough saved state that user execution can resume later as if nothing happened.
Four things happen, in order, with no software involvement:

html:ol.html:li: Reach a clean instruction boundary. The CPU cannot pause halfway through, say, a memory write — architectural state would be inconsistent. So the current instruction either finishes normally (typical for asynchronous interrupts like a timer) or is rolled back as if it never started (faults, where the instruction couldn't complete anyway and needs to be retried). A page-fault handler maps in the missing page and UHNL returns to re-execute the faulting instruction; a timer handler returns to the next instruction.

Save the minimum resume state to the kernel stack. The CPU pushes RIP (where to resume), RSP (the user stack pointer it's about to abandon), the privilege level (CS — were we in ring 3 or ring 0?), and RFLAGS. The save target is the kernel stack, whose address the CPU reads from the Task state segment that the OS set up at boot. This is a security boundary: the kernel never trusts the user stack, since user code could have corrupted it or pointed RSP at something malicious. Note what is not saved: general-purpose registers. The handler itself pushes RAX, RBX, ... if it uses them, which keeps the hardware path fast.

Jump to the handler via the Interrupt descriptor table. The CPU takes the interrupt number N, indexes entry N of the IDT (found via the IDTR register), loads the handler address into RIP, and loads the new code segment, which switches the CPU from ring 3 to ring 0. After this step, the CPU is running kernel code on the kernel stack at the right privilege level — all set up automatically.

Flush the Reorder buffer of work past the interrupt point. In an Out-of-order execution CPU the ROB may already contain instructions from after the interrupt — they've been issued speculatively because the front-end ran ahead. Those entries are discarded so the handler sees clean architectural state, as if execution had stopped exactly at the interrupt point. When UHNL returns, those instructions re-execute normally. This is the same squash machinery used for branch mispredictions in Speculative execution — and, famously, the reason Spectre-class side channels exist: speculative work leaves observable traces in caches even after the ROB is flushed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once the hardware path finishes, the OS's interrupt handler is running. Its first job is typically to save the remaining registers before doing real work — the rest is software.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Steps 2 and 3 are why an OS needs to set up the IDT and TSS before enabling interrupts. Until both structures point somewhere valid, the first interrupt would either jump to garbage or push to a user-controlled stack — both fatal.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU's lookup table from interrupt number to handler. The OS allocates the IDT in memory, fills entry N with the address of the handler for interrupt N, and points the IDTR register at the table's base. From then on the Hardware interrupt path uses the IDT to dispatch every interrupt without any further software help.

Each entry — a gate descriptor on x86 — carries enough information for the CPU to switch into the handler safely: the handler's linear address, the code segment to load (which sets the new privilege level, typically ring 0), and a type byte distinguishing interrupt gates (which mask further interrupts on entry) from trap gates (which don't). Entries 0–31 are reserved for CPU-defined exceptions (divide-by-zero, Page fault, double fault, ...); 32–255 are available for device IRQs and software interrupts.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The IDT is one of three OS-installed tables that the CPU consults transparently — the others are the GDT (segment descriptors) and the page tables. Each is a hardware contract: the OS lays bytes out in a format the silicon understands, then hands over a pointer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A small OS-allocated structure that the CPU reads to find the kernel stack pointer when it raises privilege. Its historical purpose — full task switching in hardware — is dead on modern x86-64; what survives is one essential field: RSP0, the address of the kernel stack to switch to on entry from ring 3.

During the Hardware interrupt path, the moment the CPU decides to push RIP/RSP/CS/RFLAGS, it reads RSP0 from the current TSS (located via the GDT entry pointed at by the TR register) and switches RSP to that value before pushing. If the TSS were missing or pointed at user memory, the very first interrupt would either fault while trying to push, or push the user's registers onto attacker-controlled memory. So the TSS is set up at boot, before interrupts are enabled.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: This is why a kernel needs a per-CPU TSS, not a per-process one: RSP0 is consulted on every transition into the kernel, and each CPU needs its own kernel stack to land on.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes — concepts these chips introduced live in their own atomic notes; this tree records what was novel about each implementation.
Concepts in play:

html:ul.html:li: Cache hierarchy — Pentium adopted L1/L2 caching and split the L1 into separate instruction and data caches.
Branch prediction — branch history table (BHT) and branch target buffer (BTB) combined with static prediction as a fallback on BHT miss.
Speculative execution — execute past a predicted branch, write back only after the prediction is confirmed.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch / Prefetch: read from instruction cache.
Decode 1: decode to internal format; branch prediction.
Decode 2: convert complex instructions via Microarchitecture to Micro-operations.
Execute.
Write.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end: two integer ALUs (also doing address calculations) and one floating-point ALU. The FP register file was arranged as a stack, with most FP ops operating on the top — zero-cycle stack swaps existed but still occupied a decode slot.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Estimated 30% of Pentium transistors went to legacy support, mostly the microcode ROM.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Concepts introduced:

html:ul.html:li: Out-of-order execution — instructions execute in dataflow order, commit in program order.
Reorder buffer — 40-entry ROB tracks all in-flight instructions and assists Register renaming.
Reservation station — three-instruction dispatch, up to five-issue per cycle.
CISC vs RISC decoupling — x86 in front, Micro-operations internally.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Simple and complex decoders are split because many CISC instructions translate to one or two micro-ops — the simple decoders handle those in parallel with the slow complex decoder.
40% of Pentium Pro transistors went to backward-compatibility support.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes for the PowerPC line — a RISC implementation of the same concepts covered in Superscalar architecture and Branch prediction, with a few innovations specific to this family.
Front-end: standard 4-stage pipeline. Instruction queue dispatches 3 instructions per cycle.

html:ul.html:li: Identifies unconditional branches (and resolved conditional ones) in the queue; if taken, deletes the branch instruction and starts fetching from the target — branch folding.
Untaken branches "fall out" — fall through.
No Reorder buffer-equivalent. Static scheduling.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end: one integer ALU (also handles addresses), one floating-point ALU with single-cycle fused multiply-add. For FP-heavy code the integer ALU just does addresses.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Competed with two-integer-ALU designs because simpler logic left room for 4× the L1 cache (32KB vs 8KB).

html:ul.html:li: Branch dispatch on a separate bus, so branches don't compete with non-branch dispatch.
Up to 2 non-branch instructions dispatched per cycle.
Adds a commit unit and completion queue — the PowerPC names for Reorder buffer. Dynamic scheduling with an instruction window.
Two added execution units: a system unit for misc integer ops and a load/store unit. Lighter load on the integer ALU.
FPU took hits: 3 instructions every 4 cycles (mandatory bubble); double precision (including fmadd) takes 2 cycles in execute.
Splits decode/dispatch and writeback into two stages — enables shorter clock cycles.
Up to 6 instructions dispatched per cycle.
Dynamic Branch prediction with a 512-entry BHT (2 bits per entry) and a 64-entry branch address cache (BTB).
Adds a condition-register unit sharing dispatch bus with the branch unit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end: two simple integer units + one complex; fully pipelined FPU.
Dispatch obeys Reservation station rules: in-order dispatch, execution-unit availability, ROB availability, and rename-register availability.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Shallow pipeline → small ROB and few reservation stations are acceptable. Tradeoff: lower clock speed.
The 604e doubled L1 to 64KB and added a condition-register unit, freeing the integer and branch units from condition computations.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Same 4-stage pipeline as the 603e. Two integer units instead of one; 3-cycle single-precision FP; 6-entry instruction queue, up to 4 fetched per cycle.

The 750's signature trick: a branch target instruction cache (BTIC). A traditional BTB stores the address of the branch target — you still have to re-fetch that instruction. The BTIC caches the actual first instructions at the target, eliminating the re-fetch latency. This makes Branch prediction dramatically more useful.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Did not support a SIMD instruction set.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Added a vector permute unit (VPU) and vector ALU (VALU) for SIMD; full double-precision FPU; 8-entry instruction queue.
Two contrasting approaches to using a transistor budget, well-paired as instance studies because they target the same constraints from opposite directions.
Optimizes for instruction-level parallelism. Seven-stage pipeline.

html:ol.html:li: Additional second fetching stage.

Issue stage. To avoid in-order issue stalls, the G4e uses issue queues (general / vector / float) that issue 3, 2, and 1 instruction per cycle respectively. Issue queues differ from Reservation stations in that each queue feeds a group of execution units, not a single one.
Complete stage.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Optimizes for clock speed. 20-stage pipeline — extreme depth for fast clock, with the obvious downside that mispredicted branches are very expensive.

html:ul.html:li: Needs deep buffers (126-entry Reorder buffer) to keep the fast back-end fed.
x86 decoding was too slow to keep a 20-stage pipeline fed.
The trace cache moves to L1, storing already-decoded Micro-operations traces; the raw instruction cache moves to L2.
Trace cache is indexed during Branch prediction, so speculative paths past a branch can be stored in the same trace.
Operates in two modes — execution mode (trace hit, feed micro-ops directly) and build mode (trace miss, decode and assemble while feeding forward).

fr:title.text: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pentium M, Core Duo, Core 2 Duo

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The post-Pentium-4 generations, designed under the Power wall. Each generation contributed an innovation to the concept stack.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A processor often has three names: a brand name, a microarchitecture brand name, and a microarchitecture code name.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First chip explicitly designed for low power. Innovations:

html:ul.html:li: Widened fetch buffer to 64 bytes — decodes up to 4 16-byte x86 instructions at a time.

Micro-op fusion — pairs of Micro-operations from the same x86 instruction (e.g. store-address + store-data, or load + ALU op for a read-modify) are tracked together as one queue entry until issue. See Micro-operations.
Loop detector in Branch prediction — counters watch loop trip counts; loops that iterate the same number of times each invocation can be predicted with 100% accuracy.
Indirect predictor — like a BHT but stores each branch's favorite target address.
Stack execution unit — dedicated unit with its own adder to handle push/pop ESP updates without going through the integer ALU. Stack micro-ops never reach the back-end.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first true Multicore x86: two cores on the same die, sharing L2. Innovations relative to Pentium M:

html:ul.html:li: Every decoder is capable of micro-op fusion (up to 3 fused entries into the micro-op queue per cycle).
Fuses vector SSE instructions in addition to scalars.
Improved loop detector for short loops.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Until this point, the "core" was an abstraction — front-end + back-end + L1. The Core Duo is when "core" became a thing chips have more than one of.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Theme: "more is always better" — wider decode/dispatch/issue, bigger Reorder buffer, more Reservation stations, more decode logic. Two innovations made the extra resources pay off:

html:ul.html:li: Macro fusion (front-end) — fuse pairs of x86 instructions (e.g. compare + branch) before decode. Max decode bandwidth grows to 5 x86 instructions per cycle without burning ROB or RS entries.

Memory disambiguation (back-end) — apply Speculative execution to memory aliasing. Loads can speculatively execute ahead of stores with unknown target addresses; on actual alias detected at commit time, the load and dependent ops are squashed and re-executed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The point of memory disambiguation: stores must commit in program order relative to other stores, and no load can commit ahead of an aliased store. But the rule "no load past a store-data with unknown target" forced a lot of unnecessary stalls — most unknowns aren't actually aliases. Speculate and verify.
Back-end also gained single-cycle vector ops: a 128-bit data bus to the vector ALU replaced the previous 80-bit bus, so 128-bit SSE instructions decode as a single micro-op.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A load is one micro-op because address computation and memory access are inherently serial. A store is two micro-ops (store-address + store-data) because address computation and reading the register file can happen in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: n-bit computing refers to how many bits a register can hold. 64-bit means registers hold 64 bits, 64-bit instructions means their operands are 64 bits wide.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Instruction encoding size hasn't actually changed — the PC widened because the virtual address space did.
Only integer and addressing units widened. Floating point and vector units already operated on wider registers.

html:ul.html:li: Increased dynamic range — values can range over 2^{64} instead of 2^{32}. Overflow/underflow happens at the extremes of the representable range.
64-bit address space. x86-64 currently implements 40-bit virtual addresses but the architecture allows far more.
Drawback: pointers now take up twice the cache space.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: It is not always beneficial to use 64-bit integers. If you don't need the range, the wider type wastes cache space. The default integer size on x86-64 is still 32 bits; widening requires a one-byte prefix, adding less than 10% to code size.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Art of Writing Efficient Programs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Art of Writing Efficient Programs

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Notes as I progress through the book Art of Writing Efficient Programs.
Notebook for EECS 570 taught by Prof. Naryasanamy; cross-references into Notebook: Inside the Machine (Inside the Machine) for single-core/uniprocessor topics.
The observation that as transistor dimensions shrink by a factor \alpha , you can also drop voltage by \alpha , keeping power per transistor constant:
In the Dennard era, every process shrink got more transistors at the same power budget — the foundation of decades of frequency scaling.
Post-Dennard. Below ~65nm, voltage can no longer be scaled because the supply-to-threshold ratio V_{dd}/V_{th} must stay above ~3 for transistors to switch reliably. The consequences:

html:ul.html:li: Power per transistor scales only as 1/\alpha , not 1/\alpha ^2.
Power density therefore scales as \alpha — denser chips dissipate more per unit area.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the same constraint that drove the Power wall in commercial CPUs: single-core frequency scaling stopped working, and the industry pivoted to multicore and energy-efficient architectures.

An alternative to fine-grained Locks: mark code regions as transactions that should appear atomic, execute them speculatively in parallel, and detect conflicts after the fact. Aims at the performance of fine-grained locking with the programming ergonomics of coarse-grained locking.
Two transactions conflict if they access the same memory location and at least one of them is a write. The implementation tracks per-transaction read and write sets.

html:ul.html:li: Lazy detection. Build the entire read/write set as the transaction runs; check for conflicts only at commit time. If conflict, roll back.
Eager detection. Check on every access against other transactions' sets; abort immediately on conflict.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Eager detection can piggyback on the cache coherence protocol: add read/write bits to each cache line, set them during a transaction, and let a coherence invalidation signal a conflict.
Two operations: commit and abort. Implementations:

html:ol.html:li: Lazy: stores go to a "write table" rather than memory; loads check the table first, falling back to memory. Commit writes the table back (slow); abort just clears the table (fast).

Eager (coherence-backed): stores write through but track read/write sets per cache line. Commit clears the sets (fast); abort clears the sets (also fast — work already on overwrite if not aborted before commit).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If a transaction touches more cache lines than the cache holds, the tracking overflows.
Fall back to software transactional memory (STM) on overflow — a hybrid scheme that keeps hardware fast-path for common cases.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A lock is a synchronization primitive that grants mutually exclusive access to a critical section. The implementations below trade off coherence traffic, fairness, and memory in different ways. The naive locks (Test-and-set lock and Test-and-test-and-set lock) are simple but generate O(n) coherence traffic per release; queue-based locks (Ticket lock, Array-based lock, MCS lock) scale better at the cost of extra state per waiter.

The simplest spin lock. The acquire loop spins on an atomic test_and_set: it writes LOCKED and reads the previous value in one step. If the previous value was UNLOCKED, the caller has the lock; otherwise it tries again.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Major coherence traffic. Every spinning core must acquire write permissions and invalidate everyone else on each test_and_set, so contention scales badly with the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A refinement of the Test-and-set lock that reduces coherence traffic during the spin. Threads first perform a plain load to check whether the lock looks free, and only attempt the atomic test_and_set when it does. Spinning is then read-only, so cores can share the cache line.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Spinning is a read-only operation now.

When the lock is released, the line must be invalidated on every waiter, and they all simultaneously read and try to write. The contention is shifted from steady state to release time but is still O(n) in the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A fair spin lock based on two counters. Each arriving thread takes a "ticket" by atomically incrementing next_ticket and then spins until now_serving equals its ticket. Release simply increments now_serving. Threads acquire the lock in FIFO order, which prevents starvation but does not by itself reduce contention.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock_ptr): my_ticket = fetch_and_increment(lock_ptr->next_ticket) while (lock_ptr->now_serving != my_ticket); // spin
release(lock_ptr): lock_ptr->now_serving = lock_ptr->now_serving + 1

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Still O(n) coherence traffic on release: incrementing now_serving invalidates the line on every waiter, and they all reload it to recheck their ticket.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each waiter is assigned its own slot in an array and spins only on that slot. Release just flips the next slot to "go-ahead", so the cache line touched by release is the one read by exactly one other thread. Coherence traffic per release is constant rather than O(n), at the cost of one slot per waiter.

html:ul.html:li: Give each waiter its own "slot" to wait on.
Only need to set the next slot to "go-ahead" on release.
Constant coherence traffic.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: O(n) memory for the lock array, and padding each slot to 64b (one cache line) to avoid false sharing makes that cost real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A scalable, list-based queue lock. The lock variable is a pointer to the tail of a linked list of per-thread nodes. Each thread enqueues its own node and spins on a flag in its own cache line. The releaser wakes only its direct successor, so coherence traffic per handoff is constant and memory cost is proportional only to threads currently waiting, not to the lock's fanout.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock): I->next = null predecessor = fetch_and_store(lock, I) if predecessor != nil: // some node holds the lock I->must_wait = true predecessor->next = I // predecessor must wake us repeat while I->must_wait // spin till lock is free
release(lock): if (I->next == null): // no known successor if compare_and_swap(lock, I, nil): // make sure... return // CAS succeeded; lock freed repeat while I->next == nil // spin to learn successor I->next->must_wait = false // wake successor

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a Multicore system where every core has its own Cache hierarchy, the same memory address can live in multiple caches simultaneously. A cache coherence protocol is the mechanism that keeps those copies consistent — propagating writes, invalidating stale copies, and preserving two invariants:

html:ol.html:li: Single-writer invariant: at any moment, at most one core has permission to write a given line. No other core can read or write it concurrently.
Data-value invariant: all cores see the same order of updates to a given line.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families:

html:ul.html:li: Snoop-based coherence — every cache watches a shared bus and reacts to other cores' traffic. Scales to ~8 cores.
Directory-based coherence — a structure tracks who has a copy of each line; coherence messages are point-to-point. Scales to hundreds of cores at the cost of an extra hop.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The concrete state machines are protocols like MSI protocol, MESI protocol, MOESI protocol, and DEC Firefly, each adding states to handle more cases or to optimize specific sharing patterns. A worked implementation of directory-based MESI in Murphi lives in Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi.
Worked instance: Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi implements directory-based MESI in Murphi.

The strongest practical memory model. The result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all threads were executed in some single sequential order, and the operations of each thread appear in that order in the same sequence as the program specifies.

Equivalent operational definition: imagine a single switch that, at each step, selects one thread to run its next operation. Any valid execution is the result of some sequence of such selections. There is no reordering across threads, no reordering within a thread, and no buffering that becomes visible to other threads.

Sequential consistency is what most programmers reason about by default — and what real hardware does not provide, because it forbids almost every optimization that makes a CPU fast. Relaxed consistency is what we get in exchange.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: SC does not provide guarantees between threads beyond ordering. Two threads racing on the same variable are still racing — SC just promises a consistent global timeline.

Enforcing SC purely in hardware requires loads to wait for the Store buffer to drain and stores to retire strictly in order. Both kill performance. SC-preserving compilers try to push the constraint to compile time by refusing to reorder shared accesses.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A memory model weaker than Sequential consistency that allows the hardware (and compiler) to reorder memory operations for performance, with explicit Memory fence instructions where the programmer wants to forbid reordering.

A fence is implemented with a counter — incremented on issue of each memory op, decremented on completion. The fence stalls dispatch until the counter reaches zero, which forces the Store buffer to drain before later accesses may proceed.

Real machines pick a relaxation along a spectrum: Total store order permits only store-load reordering and is what x86 ships; Relaxed memory order permits anything and pushes the ordering burden onto the programmer. Either way, the next move is to make the fences themselves cheaper, which is what Release consistency does.

A Relaxed consistency relaxation in which loads may be hoisted above prior stores, but no other reorderings are permitted. The motivation: a store can sit in the Store buffer waiting for cache, while subsequent loads — which do not touch the buffered address — proceed without stalling.

Same-core reads of a still-buffered store get the right value through Store-to-load forwarding; cross-core reads see the old value until the buffer drains. This asymmetry is the entire point of TSO's relaxation: the store buffer hides latency from the issuing thread without forcing other threads to wait. x86 implements TSO.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Under TSO, Dekker's algorithm is still broken without Memory fence — the classic example where TSO is too weak for naive software synchronization.
The IBM 370 variant is TSO without Store-to-load forwarding — it preserves global store atomicity at the cost of same-core stall-on-self-read.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Relaxed consistency relaxation in which any pair of memory operations may be reordered. RMO is the most permissive end of the consistency spectrum; the programmer recovers ordering only through explicit Memory fence instructions.
Each stronger reordering still has to be justified per case:

html:ul.html:li: Load → store — no performance gain to reorder; leave alone.
Store → store — keep ordering with a slightly larger Store buffer.
Load → load — speculate the second load while waiting for the first; the reorder is invisible if the speculation is verified.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Because every fence is expensive under RMO, the natural next move is to weaken the fences themselves into half-fences, which is what Release consistency does.

A Relaxed consistency discipline that recovers data-race freedom (see Data race) under Relaxed memory order not by issuing fewer fences, but by issuing weaker ones. The full-fence cost of Sequential consistency is replaced by a pair of half-fences placed exactly at the boundaries of critical sections — acquire on entry, release on exit.

The intuition is that correctness around a critical section only requires that work cannot leak out of it; work is free to sink into it. Half-fences encode exactly this asymmetry, which is why they are the right primitive for lock-based and lock-free synchronization alike.

A Memory fence across which no memory operation may move in either direction. Prior accesses must become globally visible before any subsequent access may issue. x86's mfence is the canonical example — the hammer that drains the Store buffer and serializes all prior memory operations against all subsequent ones.

Full fences are the default ordering primitive under Sequential consistency — every memory operation behaves as if a full fence sat on either side. They are also the only fence one needs to reason about correctness, which is why they are the building block for the half-fences of Release consistency.
A Memory fence that constrains motion in only one direction, splitting the symmetric guarantee of a Full fence into two cheaper primitives:

html:ul.html:li: Acquire — operations after the fence may not move above it. Used at the entry to a critical section: subsequent reads and writes cannot be hoisted before the lock is held.
Release — operations before the fence may not move below it. Used at the exit of a critical section: prior writes cannot be deferred past the unlock.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The asymmetry is exactly the freedom one wants around a critical section: expanding the section is fine — work may sink into it (past acquire) or rise into it (before release) — but work may not leak out of it. This is the basis of Release consistency and the reason C++ std::atomic exposes memory_order_acquire / memory_order_release as its core primitives.
C++ std::atomic compiles to half-fences in the right places, which is why it is the standard primitive for portable lock-free code.

A FIFO of retired-but-not-yet-globally-visible stores that decouples instruction commit from cache write. When a store reaches the head of the Reorder buffer and commits, its value has not yet reached L1 — commit only hands the store off to the store buffer. The actual cache write, called drain, happens asynchronously and may be delayed by cache misses, coherence traffic, or memory-ordering rules.

Without this split, the ROB would have to stall on a cache miss for every store. With it, once a store is known to be non-speculative, the front-end moves on while the store buffer absorbs whatever latency the memory hierarchy imposes. Commit and drain are therefore two distinct moments in the lifetime of a store:

html:ul.html:li: Commit — the store is architecturally real. Younger speculation cannot squash it, but other cores do not see it yet.
Drain — the value is written to L1 and becomes visible to the coherence protocol, and therefore to other cores.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Between these two moments the value lives only in the store buffer. Younger same-core loads can still observe it through Store-to-load forwarding, but other cores cannot — which is the structural reason Total store order permits store-load reordering across cores. A Memory fence like mfence forces the buffer to drain so that subsequent operations see a consistent global view.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Retirement bandwidth (ROB commits per cycle) and store bandwidth (drain rate to L1) are separate throughput limits. A workload that retires stores faster than the cache can absorb them eventually backs up the ROB anyway, but the store buffer absorbs short bursts at no cost.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mechanism by which a younger load on the same core observes the value of an older store that is still sitting in the Store buffer and has not yet drained to L1. The load-store unit checks the store buffer for any in-flight store to the same address; if one exists, the load's data comes directly from the buffer entry rather than from the cache, satisfying the same-core sequential semantics that the program expects.

Forwarding is the reason the commit/drain split does not break the single-core illusion. Within one thread, a store followed by a load to the same address must read the just-stored value — and would, on a machine without a store buffer, because the cache is the single source of truth. With a store buffer, the cache is briefly stale relative to architectural state; forwarding patches that hole.

The structural consequence shows up in Total store order: store-load reordering across cores is permitted precisely because forwarding makes same-core reads see the new value, while cross-core reads continue to see the old one until drain. This asymmetry is the entire point of TSO's relaxation — the store buffer can hide latency from the issuing thread without forcing other threads to wait.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Address matching in the store buffer is partial: most implementations check enough low-order bits to detect a hit but fall back to a stall (or a re-execution) on aliasing or partial-overlap cases. Reliable wide-then-narrow forwarding is one of the harder corners of memory disambiguation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An instruction that forces an ordering constraint between memory operations on either side of it. Fences exist because Relaxed consistency hardware reorders accesses aggressively for performance, and the programmer occasionally needs a point in the instruction stream past which prior accesses must be globally visible before subsequent ones may issue. A fence is the explicit knob for "wait until the world catches up here."
The x86 family carries three:

html:ul.html:li: sfence — store fence. All prior stores must drain from the Store buffer before any later store may issue. Used to publish a buffer whose writes must be visible to consumers in order.

lfence — load fence. All prior loads must complete before any later load issues. Less commonly needed under TSO, but useful as a speculation barrier (Spectre mitigation cases) and on architectures with load-load reordering.
mfence — full fence. Drains the entire store buffer and serializes all prior memory operations against all subsequent ones. The hammer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: What a fence actually does in hardware is force the store buffer to drain (or wait for it to drain) rather than just waiting for Reorder buffer commit. This is the distinction worth keeping straight: ROB commit makes a store architecturally real but invisible to other cores; the fence is what makes it visible. The cost is whatever latency the cache hierarchy adds — fences are expensive precisely because they undo the latency-hiding the Store buffer exists to provide.

Under Sequential consistency every memory operation behaves as if a full fence sat on either side of it, which is why SC is fast to reason about and slow to implement. Real-world consistency models pick relaxations and require fences only at the points the programmer marks as ordering-sensitive — acquire and release Half fences (see Release consistency) are the common compromise.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A fence does not flush caches or invalidate anything — coherence traffic does that. A fence only constrains the issue order observed by the rest of the system.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two accesses to the same memory location, from different threads, where at least one is a write and there is no happens-before relationship between them. The happens-before relation is what synchronization primitives like Locks and atomics induce — when one access happens before another, they are not racing.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Competing for the same lock is not a data race. Whichever thread acquires first happens before the next; the access inside each critical section is sequentially ordered.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Data-race freedom (DRF) is the implicit contract behind every relaxed memory model: if your program has no data races, Relaxed consistency hardware behaves as if it were Sequential consistency. Conversely, in the presence of a data race, the result is undefined by the language and the model.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Writing data-race-free programs is hard. Treating every shared access as a synchronization point destroys performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: SC-preserving compilers approach: forbid the compiler from reordering shared accesses, leaving the hardware free to do TSO-style reordering. The optimization most disrupted is common-subexpression elimination across shared accesses — solved by inserting a "monitor" check on the coherence bit between expressions.

Hardware approach. Treat all pages as local/safe by default and extend page protection bits to the thread level: a non-owning thread accessing the page triggers a fault, exposing the data race at runtime.

Parallelism extracted from within a single thread by executing independent instructions concurrently. The instruction stream is sequential as written, but a sufficiently aggressive back-end can issue, execute, and complete instructions in a different order than they appear, as long as data dependencies are respected and the visible architectural state still looks in-order.
The machinery a CPU spends on ILP is the bulk of its area and complexity:

html:ul.html:li: Out-of-order execution — issue instructions whenever their operands are ready, not in program order.
Register renaming — break false dependencies by mapping the same architectural register name to different physical registers.
Reservation stations + Reorder buffer — hold dispatched instructions while waiting on operands, then commit them in original order.
Speculative execution and speculation — keep the pipeline fed across control-flow boundaries by predicting which way a branch will go.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: ILP runs into hard limits: dependency chains, branch mispredictions, and the quadratic scaling of issue-queue wakeup-select all cap how wide a single-threaded window can grow. Beyond that ceiling, more silicon stops buying more performance, which is the structural reason the industry pivoted to Thread-level parallelism for throughput-bound workloads.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: ILP is what classic uniprocessor texts mean by "out-of-order superscalar execution"; see Notebook: Inside the Machine for the unwound history.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Parallelism extracted by running multiple independent threads concurrently and switching between them whenever any one of them stalls. Where Instruction-level parallelism mines a single instruction stream for hidden concurrency, TLP gives up on that ambition and instead assumes that enough independent work exists in the program (or workload) that some thread is always ready to make progress.

TLP makes the per-thread microarchitecture cheap. There is no need for a deep issue window, aggressive speculation, or CAM-based wakeup-select: if the current thread can't proceed, the scheduler picks another. The cost shifts from per-instruction machinery to context-holding — registers, program counters, and stack pointers for every resident thread must live somewhere fast.
Two dominant implementation styles:

html:ul.html:li: Coarse-grained TLP. Conventional multicore CPUs and SMT: a handful of threads share a core, switched on long-latency events or interleaved cycle-by-cycle.
