Massive TLP. GPUs keep dozens of warps resident per GPU architecture SM and switch on any stall. The GPU register scoreboard is the readiness check that makes this cheap.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TLP fails when the workload genuinely has only one thread of useful work, or when the threads contend on shared state (locks, Cache coherence traffic). It wins overwhelmingly on data-parallel kernels where the threads are independent by construction.

The two strategies a microarchitecture can use to keep arithmetic units busy in the face of memory latency and dependencies. Instruction-level parallelism mines parallelism from within a single instruction stream; Thread-level parallelism mines it across many. Every modern design is a position on this axis, and the position determines the rest of the microarchitecture.

The choice is not really a choice — it is forced by the workload. ILP works only when the single thread has independent work to find; once dependency chains and branch mispredictions saturate the issue window, no amount of silicon spent on reorder buffers, wakeup logic, or speculation buys more throughput. TLP works only when many independent threads exist; throwing more warps at a single sequential dependency chain accomplishes nothing.
The downstream consequences split everything else:

html:ul.html:li: Control overhead. ILP machines spend most of their area on Out-of-order execution machinery — Reorder buffer, Reservation stations, Register renaming, CAM-based wakeup-select. TLP machines spend almost none; the schedule is "pick any ready thread."
Caches. ILP needs large caches because a single stalled thread blocks the whole pipeline. TLP can afford small caches because the latency is hidden by switching threads.

Register file. ILP keeps one (renamed) register set hot. TLP keeps every resident thread's register set hot simultaneously — GPU register files are an order of magnitude larger than CPU register files for this reason.
Readiness tracking. ILP pays O(N²) for CAM-based wakeup-select across N in-flight instructions; TLP pays O(1) per instruction via a per-register scoreboard.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The slogan: CPUs spend a lot of silicon to find parallelism in one thread; GPUs spend almost none because they have many threads. Everything downstream — pipeline depth, cache hierarchy, scheduling policy, energy per operation — falls out of which side of this axis the chip sits on.

Single instruction, multiple data. Extend the Fetch-execute loop so that one fetch+decode applies the same operation to a vector of operands. The fetch-decode overhead amortizes over the vector width; loop bookkeeping (increment, compare, branch) is replaced by a single vector op.
Concretely, a vector ISA adds:

html:ul.html:li: A vector register file — wider than scalar registers; specify the data width per element and total bits per register.
Vector load/store instructions for moving contiguous chunks of memory to/from vector registers efficiently.
Vector predication (masking) — apply the op only where the mask is true, allowing data-dependent control flow without scalar branches.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Trade-offs:

html:ul.html:li: Need the array length to be divisible by the vector width for perfect speedup; tail handling is annoying.
Vector pipelines cannot do Forwarding cheaply — would need O(n^2) connections to every ALU.
Wider hardware vectors can be supported on narrower hardware by microcoded decomposition; backward compatibility falls out for free.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In practice, vector code is generated by:

html:ol.html:li: Intrinsic functions (architecture-specific C builtins).
Vector data types in a language extension.
External libraries (BLAS, etc.).
Compiler auto-vectorization.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Aliasing: modifying a vector you're also reading from causes a race within the operation.
SIMD is hardware-driven data parallelism. The GPU descendant is SIMT, which softens many of SIMD's rigidities by giving each lane its own program counter and active mask.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A GPU sacrifices single-thread latency for aggregate throughput. The whole machine is built around the Thread-level parallelism design point — keep many threads resident, switch on every stall, spend almost no silicon on per-instruction control. The opposite end of the axis from a CPU:

The GPU equivalent of a core is a streaming multiprocessor (SM). Each SM executes the same instruction across many threads in lockstep — the SIMT programming model — and uses a per-register GPU register scoreboard rather than CPU-style wakeup-select to decide which warp can issue next.

Single instruction, multiple threads. A GPU streaming multiprocessor issues one instruction per cycle to a group of threads — a Warp scheduling — but each thread within the group keeps its own program counter and register state. The hardware enforces lockstep execution within the warp while preserving the illusion of independent threads to the programmer.

SIMT is the GPU's answer to a question the Vector ISA / SIMD world struggled with: how do you get the throughput of wide vector execution without forcing the programmer to manually vectorize? Two mechanisms make this work.

When threads in a warp take different branches of a conditional, the warp does not split into smaller warps. Instead, the active mask selects which lanes execute each branch; lanes on the inactive side run dummy instructions until the warp reconverges at the post-conditional join point. The programmer writes ordinary scalar code with if/else, and the hardware silently masks the inactive lanes.

This is the chief ergonomic win over SIMD: the compiler does not have to prove that all lanes will take the same path. The cost is that divergent code pays for both sides — a 32-wide warp where 16 threads take the if-branch and 16 take the else-branch executes both branches sequentially with half the lanes idle each time.

Threads are assigned to warps based on warp-ID, but the hardware and compiler are free to regroup. JKNL re-bundles threads with similar control-flow trajectories into the same warp so that future branches stay non-divergent. This is a class of optimization that pure SIMD cannot express — a SIMD ISA fixes the lane assignment at compile time.

The hardware cost of SIMT relative to SIMD is the per-thread PC and register state. The benefit is that a single binary runs efficiently across a wide range of dynamic control flow, which is why the model has dominated GPU programming since CUDA. The scoreboard mechanism described in GPU register scoreboard operates per-warp rather than per-thread precisely because SIMT treats the warp as the unit of issue.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: SIMT is sometimes described as "SIMD with a per-lane PC," which captures the implementation but understates the programming-model impact: vectorization becomes a runtime, not a compile-time, decision.
How do GPUs deal with cache misses given they don't have OoO machinery?
Fast context-switching between warps. When a warp stalls on memory, the SM switches to another runnable warp with already-resident state. Enough warps means the SM is always busy.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a CPU, context switching is expensive — registers must be saved to memory. On a GPU, each SM has an enormous register file that holds the registers of all resident warps simultaneously, so a context switch is just changing which warp the scheduler picks. This is the concrete payoff of the Thread-level parallelism design choice: latency hiding becomes a scheduling problem instead of a microarchitecture problem.

The readiness-tracking structure that lets a GPU issue instructions out of program order without any of the CAM machinery a CPU needs. A scoreboard is a small table — typically one busy bit per architectural register — that records whether some in-flight instruction is still computing a value that something else might want to read.

The protocol is trivial. When an instruction issues, it sets the busy bit on its destination register. When it writes back, it clears the bit. Before any instruction can read its sources, the hardware checks the corresponding bits: if any source is busy, the instruction (or its entire warp) waits. The check is a single table lookup per source, so the per-instruction cost is O(1) regardless of how many other instructions are in flight.

This is enough because GPUs lean on Thread-level parallelism rather than Instruction-level parallelism. When a warp's next instruction reads a busy register, the GPU architecture scheduler does not stall — it picks a different ready warp from the dozens of resident warps and runs that one instead. The scoreboard's job is only to answer "is this warp ready? yes/no," which is far cheaper than the broadcast/compare a CPU Issue queue wakeup-select does to find dependency satisfaction in a single thread's instruction window.

The size difference falls out of this. CPU issue queues hold tens of entries; GPU instruction windows can hold many warps' worth of in-flight work, because every additional warp costs a row in the scoreboard and a register-file allocation, not a quadratic CAM expansion. See ILP vs TLP for why the design fork sits where it does.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The scoreboard's simplicity is what lets GPUs spend their transistor budget on ALUs and the Register file instead of control logic. It is one of the most visible places where the Thread-level parallelism design choice cashes out as silicon savings.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A warp is 32 threads on an NVIDIA GPU executing in lockstep — one instruction issued per cycle to all 32. The SM's warp scheduler picks which resident warp to run on each cycle.

The point of the scheduler: hide stalls. When a warp issues a memory operation that misses cache, it can't run for hundreds of cycles. With many resident warps, the scheduler always has a runnable one. Aggregate throughput stays high even though individual-warp latency is awful.

Occupancy is the ratio of active warps to maximum allowed warps per SM. Higher occupancy means more candidates for the scheduler to switch to, which means more latency hiding. Low occupancy means the SM stalls because every resident warp is waiting on the same memory transaction.
For latency-bound workloads (and most GPU code is), maximizing occupancy is the first-order optimization.

When threads in a warp take different branches of a conditional, the SM must serialize: execute one branch with its threads active and the others masked off, then the other. The lanes on the inactive path execute dummy instructions, wasting their compute slots.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Structure code so threads in a warp follow the same control flow when possible.
Warp specialization: have the compiler group threads with the same control-flow pattern into the same warp.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Graphics Processing Cluster (GPC) is a top-level hardware partition inside an NVIDIA GPU. It's one level up from the SM in the chip's hierarchy.
As of Hopper, SMs located in the same GPC are connected via an SM-to-SM network, enabling DSMEM.
NVIDIA's hardware-level term for what CUDA programmers call a thread block.

A CTA is a group of threads that execute on the same Streaming Multiprocessor (SM), can synchronize via barriers (__syncthreads()), and share fast on-chip shared memory (Shared memory (GPU)). Threads within a CTA are scheduled and executed in groups of 32 called warps.

Shared memory on a GPU is a small (per-block) on-chip scratchpad with latency comparable to L1. Software-managed — the programmer explicitly stages data through it, the hardware does not cache for them. Used to:

html:ul.html:li: Avoid non-coalesced global accesses by reorganizing data layouts on the fly.
Reuse loaded data across threads within a block.
Communicate between threads within a block.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shared memory is divided into banks (typically 32). Each bank can service one access per cycle. If two threads in a Warp scheduling access different addresses in the same bank, the accesses serialize — a bank conflict.
Three behaviors when warp threads access shared memory:

html:ul.html:li: Different banks: full parallelism. No conflict.
Same bank, same word: hardware broadcasts the word to all threads. No conflict.
Same bank, different words: serialize.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: How to avoid bank conflicts?
Change the access pattern so threads hit different banks (often by transposing or by adding padding to break the stride that causes the conflict).

The GPU memory hierarchy is software-managed at L1 (shared memory) and coherent only at L2 — by restricting L1 to local-to-block scope, the architecture sidesteps the cost of a global Cache coherence protocol on a thousand-way-parallel chip.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware optimization on GPUs that combines simultaneous memory accesses by threads in a Warp scheduling into a single transaction — when the accessed addresses fall in the same cache line.
Global memory latency on a GPU is 400–800 cycles. Coalescing is the primary way to keep the SM fed:

html:ul.html:li: If all 32 threads in a warp access addresses within one cache line: one memory transaction, full bandwidth.
If addresses span multiple cache lines: the hardware serializes into multiple transactions; bandwidth drops proportionally.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Address permutations within a cache line don't hurt utilization — coalescing is about which lines are touched, not which exact bytes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Coalescing is why memory layout matters so much for GPU code. The standard idiom is structure-of-arrays (SoA) instead of array-of-structures (AoS): threads accessing the same field of consecutive records hit consecutive bytes, which coalesce; threads accessing the same struct don't.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Should you always cache on the GPU?

No. If you're memory-bound and have no Spatial locality, cache is wasted on data you won't reuse. CUDA exposes the option to disable L1 caching for these cases, which lets misaligned loads transfer only the bytes needed instead of full cache lines.
Use GPUs for parallel parts where throughput dominates. Use CPUs for sequential parts where latency dominates — the ILP vs TLP axis is the right frame for deciding which.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Application-Specific Integrated Circuits fuse multiple operations into hardware that a general-purpose pipeline would have to schedule sequentially. The motivating intuition: if you frequently multiply-accumulate, the multiply and the add each traverse the entire pipeline separately. A dedicated multiply-accumulate ALU does both in one cycle.
The cost-of-operation gap is large — 32-bit vs 8-bit addition has roughly an order of magnitude difference in energy per op. ASICs win by matching width to need.

html:ul.html:li: Vector register file each register is 8x128 vector registers with 8 horizontal lanes and 128 vertical lanes. There are typically 64 of these registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A core component of the TPU architecture responsible for elementwise operations.

html:ul.html:li: 2D SIMD execution, where each of 1024 sites execute same program in lockstep.
Each site contains 4 ALUs, enabling 4096 FLOPs.
Fed a 8x128 Vector Register in one cycle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A core component of the TPU architecture responsible for performing matrix multiplication via a systolic array.

html:ul.html:li: 256x256 totaling 65,536 multiply accumulate units i.e. each unit can multiply and add in one cycle.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Can only do 256x256 matrix multiplication, so the order is
Tiling the matrix to load from HBM to VMEM
Compiler tiles from VMEM to 256x256 tiles to load into MXU.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Note the input are offset so that the result from the first column can be accumulated int the second column. Can compute matmul in 2n - 1 cycles.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Notes on guest lecture by Yufeng Gu titled Architecture and System Co-Design for Scalable Large Language Model Inference
TFLOPs increasing much faster than DRAM bandwidth -> Hardware scaling is hitting the memory wall.
Data center power consumption also increasing \approx 10% a year.

html:p.html:strong: GPUs are under low utilization for LLM inference

html:ul.html:li: Prefix stage: parallel
Decoding stage: sequential -> bottleneck!

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As batch size increases, the KV-cache dominates memory usage.

html:ul.html:li: KV cache size grows with context length.
Tradeoff between context length and batch size.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Unlike fully-connected cannot batch self-attention because it depends on the individual prompts.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Recall: operational intensity is \frac {\text {compute throughput}}{\text {memory bandwidth}}

html:p.html:strong: Inference much lower operational intensity than GEMM!

html:ul.html:li: Inference only doing matrix \times row
If embedding dim is D, then this is only D ops / byte.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Processing in memory (PIM) enables faster memory bandwidth.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Has lower memory capacity (fewer GB) due to added processing units (PU).
Use Compute eXpress Link (CXL) to do memory expansion to get high memory bandwidth + expend memory capacity.

Put high use operations i.e. vector multiplication, element-wise multiplication in PIM module (99% usage). Put more complicated operations on PNM accelerator. TODO: ISAs for each specified on slides (not available yet).
CENT supports inter and intra-device communication via connecting communication controller to shared buffer and CXL port.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Communcation primitives: multicast and gather

html:ul.html:li: Multicast: send to all CXL devices.
Gather: receive data sequentially from all CXL devices.
Multicsat-gather -> tensor parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Peer-to-peer: can also support pipeline parallelism.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: For fast communication, use tensor parallelism i.e. if connected via NVLink. If slower communication use pipeline parallelism. Can mix.
Longer context -> better performance for PIM (TODO: why?). HBM + PIM does not scale. Cannot arbitrarily add PIM.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The network connecting cores, L2/L3 banks, and memory controllers on a Multicore chip. The same abstractions that govern off-chip networks (topology, routing, flow control) apply, but the constraints are different: wires are abundant but die area is scarce, latency is dominated by hops rather than propagation, and the network is part of the Cache coherence critical path.
The measurements used to compare network topologies and traffic patterns.

html:ul.html:li: Degree: number of links at each node. A proxy for router complexity — each port costs a buffer, crossbar input, state machine.

Bisection bandwidth: bandwidth across a cut that splits the network into two equal-sized halves. The minimum over all such cuts. A proxy for peak achievable throughput, especially for uniformly random traffic.
Diameter: maximum hop distance between any two nodes. The worst-case hop count.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Bisection bandwidth is less useful for on-chip networks than off-chip because global on-chip wiring is plentiful.
Ring bisection is 2 (two cuts to bisect); torus bisection is 4.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count: hops between two specific nodes. The average over a traffic distribution is a useful proxy for latency.
Maximum channel load: the most-loaded link's load relative to per-node injection bandwidth, for a given traffic pattern. The proxy for sustained throughput.
Path diversity: number of distinct shortest paths between two nodes. Higher path diversity allows adaptive routing to spread load.
Zero-load latency: average hop count with no contention.
Saturation throughput: the injection rate at which latency curves upward exponentially.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Hop count is not the same as latency. Per-hop pipeline depth and link propagation matter — more short hops can beat fewer slow ones. Router pipeline depth isn't always known in advance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families for on-chip networks.

html:ul.html:li: Direct — every terminal node (core, memory controller) is associated with a router. Most on-chip designs are direct because area is precious and there's no point spending a router with no compute attached. The "k-ary n-cube" family (Ring topology, Mesh topology, Torus topology) is direct.

Indirect — routers may exist that connect only to other routers, with no terminal attached. Butterfly network and Clos network are indirect; a router in their middle stages just shuffles bits between input and output stages.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Indirect networks can shorten the diameter for the same node count, but they pay for extra silicon area for routers that don't serve traffic locally. Often the right call off-chip; usually the wrong call on-chip.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest indirect network: an n \times m grid of switch points connecting n inputs to m outputs. Any input can be connected to any free output simultaneously — non-blocking.

html:ul.html:li: One hop per transit (minimum possible).
Serializes if multiple senders target the same receiver.
Switch area grows as O(n \cdot m) — the dominant cost as systems grow.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Crossbar router area scales as the square of the port count, which is why high-radix routers are dramatically more expensive than low-radix ones.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Crossbars are common as the inner switch of larger structured networks (Clos network, Fat tree) — large flat crossbars are impractical at scale, but small crossbars are cheap.

A direct network where each node connects to two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Bidirectional rings allow either direction of travel; unidirectional rings allow only one. The simplest topology to implement and reason about.
In the k-ary n-cube family, a ring is a k-ary 1-cube.

html:ul.html:li: Degree 2 (every node).
Diameter \lfloor k/2 \rfloor for a bidirectional k-node ring.
Bisection bandwidth 2 — cut the ring at exactly two links.
Hardware overhead very low: routers have two ports.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Worst-case hop count is O(k). Doesn't scale beyond a small number of nodes.
Despite the poor scaling, rings are common in commercial CPUs (Intel's L3 ring bus on Sandy/Ivy Bridge) because they're cheap and 8–16 cores is enough.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A 2D grid of nodes with neighbors connected on the four compass directions, no wrap-around. The dominant on-chip topology in commercial designs (Xeon Phi, recent server x86 chips) because it lays out naturally on a 2D die.

html:ul.html:li: Interior nodes degree 4; edge nodes degree 3; corners degree 2.
Average hop count for an even-k mesh: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{3}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/4 — twice that of a Torus topology.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Non-uniform node degree. Edge nodes carry less traffic than they could, while interior nodes bottleneck on traffic flowing through them.

Mesh dominates Torus topology in practice despite the topology metrics being worse, because torus wrap-around wires are physically long and force expensive folding to equalize link lengths. Manufacturing wins over theoretical performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Mesh topology with wrap-around links connecting the ends of each dimension. In the k-ary n-cube classification, a k-ary n-cube. Uniform node degree (every node has degree 2n), uniform load distribution, half the hop count of a mesh.

html:ul.html:li: Every node has degree 2n.
Average hop count for even k: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{4}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/8 — half that of a Mesh topology.
Good path diversity: many shortest paths between any two nodes.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Wrap-around links are physically long. To equalize wire delays, the torus must be folded, doubling effective wire lengths and link area. The wrap-around also doubles the link count versus a mesh of the same dimensions.
Despite the theoretical advantage, mesh wins on real chips. Tori show up in HPC interconnects (Cray) where the wires can run on dedicated cables.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Variants:

html:ul.html:li: k-ary n-cube: k nodes along each of n dimensions, all ends wrapped.
Asymmetric: (i, j, k)-ary — different node count per dimension.
Mesh: same as torus but without wrap-around.
Ring: k-ary 1-cube.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A multistage indirect network. A k-ary n-fly has k^n terminal nodes connected through n stages of k \times k crossbar switches. At each stage, the routing decision selects which of k sub-groups to enter; after n stages, every terminal can reach every other.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count n - 1.
Under uniform random traffic, maximum channel load is 1 (perfectly balanced).
Channel load drops further for non-uniform traffic that respects the natural partitioning.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Zero path diversity. There is exactly one path between any source and destination. A single hot link wedges traffic.
Add shuffle stages that permute paths after each routing stage, so the same source-destination pair can be reached by multiple routes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A three-stage indirect network parameterized by (m, n, r): r input switches and r output switches each of size n \times m, with m middle-stage switches each r \times r. Total terminal nodes N = nr. Non-blocking when m \geq 2n - 1.

html:ul.html:li: Number of total switching elements scales as N^{1.5} versus N^2 for a flat Crossbar while preserving non-blocking.
Path diversity is m — multiple distinct paths between any source and destination.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: There are rmn input-switch elements, mr^2 middle-switch elements, and rmn output-switch elements: S = 2rmn + mr^2. With r = N/n: S = 2Nm + m(N/n)^2. Choosing n = \sqrt {N} and m \geq 2n - 1 = O(\sqrt {N}): S = 2mN + 2m\sqrt {N} = O(N^{1.5}).
A folded Clos network — input and output stages combined, making n input ports and m output ports bidirectional — is structurally the same as a Fat tree.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A tree topology with bandwidth that doubles at every level toward the root. Each interior switch has logical degree 4, but as you climb the tree, link width doubles to absorb the combined traffic of its subtree — preserving bisection bandwidth.

Structurally, a fat tree is a folded Clos network: the input and output stages of a Clos are merged into a single bidirectional stage at each level of the tree, and the middle stage becomes the higher levels of the tree.

html:ul.html:li: Each switch has logical degree 4 (2 down, 2 up — with link doubling).
Bisection bandwidth scales linearly with the number of leaves.
Path diversity grows toward the root.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Fat trees are the dominant topology for off-chip HPC interconnects (InfiniBand fat-tree clusters). Less common on-chip because the doubling links are hard to lay out on a 2D die.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a topology and a packet's source and destination, which path through the network does it take? Three classes of answer:

html:ul.html:li:
Greedy (shortest-path) — always pick the shortest available route.
(+) Minimizes latency under light load.
(-) Overloads central links; worst-case throughput poor.

Uniform / oblivious random — pick a direction at random.
(+) Spreads load; avoids systematic hotspots.
(-) Wastes bandwidth on non-minimal paths; latency unpredictable.

Adaptive — at each hop, pick the outgoing link with the lowest local congestion.
(+) Dynamically balances load; best worst-case throughput.
(-) Routers need congestion state; risk of livelock without care.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Tornado traffic on an 8-node bidirectional ring: node i sends to (i+3) \bmod 8.
Greedy: all packets travel counter-clockwise. Bidirectional bandwidth wasted; \frac {1}{3} utilization.
Uniform: randomly clockwise/counter-clockwise. Counter-clockwise gets \frac {2}{3} utilization, clockwise \frac {2}{5}; worst-case \frac {2}{5}.
Adaptive: sends \frac {5}{8} counter-clockwise to match traffic exactly. Best.
Adaptive routing tends to need Network backpressure signaling to know where congestion is, and care to avoid livelock when many adaptive decisions chase each other.

Oblivious strategies like Dimension-order routing and Valiant's routing algorithm give deterministic guarantees (deadlock-free, bounded path length) at the cost of giving up the load-balancing of true adaptive routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An oblivious Network routing strategies for k-ary n-cube networks (Mesh topology, Torus topology): always exhaust the offset in one dimension completely before changing to the next. E.g. in 2D mesh routing, route X-then-Y — never turn back to X after starting Y.

The dimension order prevents routing deadlock: cycles in the channel dependency graph are impossible because no packet ever requests a channel in a "lower" dimension once it has started using a higher one. The dependency graph is therefore acyclic, and the standard cycle-equals-deadlock argument applies.

html:ul.html:li: (+) Provably deadlock-free without virtual channels.
(-) Zero path diversity. Hot spots can't be routed around.
(-) Unaware of current network state — oblivious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The standard remedy for the path-diversity problem is Valiant's routing algorithm, which keeps the deadlock-freedom of dimension-order routing while spreading load with a randomized intermediate hop.
An oblivious Network routing strategies that achieves provably optimal worst-case throughput by introducing randomness in path selection:

html:ol.html:li: For a packet going from s to d, randomly pick an intermediate node w.
Route s \to w using Dimension-order routing.
Eject the packet at w, then re-inject it.
Route w \to d using Dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To preserve deadlock-freedom, the intermediate node must remove the packet from the network before re-injecting — otherwise the channel dependencies of the two halves could interlock.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Restricting w to lie in the sub-quadrant defined by s and d keeps paths near-minimal while still spreading load — the practical compromise between guaranteed throughput and added latency.

Valiant's algorithm trades average-case latency (paths are longer) for worst-case throughput (any traffic pattern looks roughly uniform once randomized). The right call for adversarial workloads; overkill for uniform traffic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Routing deadlock occurs when a packet cannot make progress because the buffer at its next link is full, and the packet holding that buffer is itself blocked waiting on a buffer this packet holds — a cycle in the channel dependency graph.
Two structural responses:

html:ol.html:li: Avoid deadlock by construction. Dimension-order routing enforces an ordering on dimension transitions so the dependency graph is acyclic by design.

Detect and recover from deadlock. Allow all turns. Detect cycles via timeouts. Drain stuck packets onto a backup network (a "slow channel" — physical, or more commonly an escape virtual channel) that uses dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The relationship to other related concepts:

html:ul.html:li: Backpressure (Network backpressure) is the signaling mechanism that lets a router downstream tell upstream it is congested, before the upstream actually fills the link.
Adaptive routing avoids deadlock locally; backpressure lets adaptive routing react to remote congestion.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A signaling mechanism that lets a downstream router tell upstream "I am congested — slow down" before its buffers actually overflow. With backpressure information, adaptive routing can route around remote congestion, not just local.

The mechanism propagates: a congested link signals back to its sender, which then knows its next link will fill, which signals further back. Useful information flows in the opposite direction of the data.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Acting only on local state can cause livelock — packets bounce between hotspots, never converging.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backpressure breaks the livelock by giving local routers global-ish information without an actual global view.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Backpressure is mechanism; adaptive routing is policy. The two together are what real high-radix on-chip networks use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The on-chip network connects each core (with its L1) and the L2 cache banks (which may be shared or private). Each L2 bank has a cache controller (manages Cache coherence) and, at memory boundaries, a memory controller (talks to DRAM).
Two cache-organization choices interact with the network design:

html:ul.html:li: Private L2 caches: each tile owns its L2 slice; cross-tile traffic happens only on private-L2 miss. (+) Lower hit latency; less interconnect traffic. (-) Replicated data; more off-chip traffic on misses.
Shared L2 cache: a single logical L2 split into banks, with each address mapped to a home bank. (+) Better utilization, no replication. (-) Every L1 miss goes on the network.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The interface to the network sits at the cache controller:

html:ul.html:li: Miss status handling register (MSHR): on a cache miss, records what request the cache is waiting on. Matched against the reply when it arrives.
Transaction status handling register (TSHR): tracks outstanding memory transactions to off-chip memory from the directory cache.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Memory controllers are commonly placed at the chip perimeter, near the I/O pads. A memory controller can share a router with a core/cache tile (saves area; needs arbitration) or have its own (more area; no contention).
Hardware-managed shared memory is one design point; another is explicit message passing, where software (not hardware) is responsible for moving data between processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Links. Thicker wires with larger spacing → lower resistance, higher speed, better energy efficiency. The wire budget determines how many ports you can afford per router.

Routers. Each port costs a buffer, a crossbar input, state logic, and a link to its neighbor. Crossbar area scales as the square of port count, so high-radix routers are dramatically expensive. Rings have very simple routers but poor performance.

Floorplan. Tori need to be folded to equalize wire lengths, doubling effective wire length; meshes don't. Tori also have twice as many links as meshes, which may force narrower links and lower per-link bandwidth.
Folding can also equalize path lengths inside a network at the cost of increasing average latency.
Concentration allocates more cores per router. (-) Decreases throughput per node. (+) Programs with high locality do little remote communication, so concentration is a good fit there.
General topologies aren't always optimal. Two construction strategies:

html:ul.html:li: Splitting: start with a large crossbar; split into smaller switches until constraints are met.
Merging: start with many small switches and combine.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Hierarchical topologies use one topology within clusters and another across clusters — common when traffic has locality.
Formal notebook for my journey in Stanford's CS336 as part of my LLM self-study endeavors.

Trivially, we can do character-based tokenization i.e. mapping each character to a unique number. For example, \mathsf {chr}(97) = \text {``a''} and \mathsf {ord}(\text {``a''}) = 97. The compression ratio will be > 1 because each token corresponds to a character potentially consisting of more than one byte.

html:ol.html:li: Very large vocabulary
Many characters are quite rare.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: There is a fundamental tradeoff between compression ratio and vocabulary size. As an example, suppose we map every sequence of n bytes to a token. This would achieve a compression ratio of n. However, this would require 256^n tokens in our vocabulary.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Problem 2 was not entirely straight-forward to me. This actually reminds me of an idea from my information theory course, where we can assume we are given a "prior" over human language then we want to map low-probability characters to large strings in order to minimize the expected length (see huffman coding).

In byte-based tokenization, each byte is a token achieving a compression ratio of exactly 1, where a byte \mapsto [0, 256] . This is very elegant in the sense that our vocabulary size is fixed at 256.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem: However, with this, the length of our encoding is # of bytes in the string, which will become too long.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In word-based tokenization, we simply split the string into the corresponding words then map each of these to a unique integer.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem: The vocabulary size is "unbounded" i.e. if we see a word we have not seen before then we need to extend the vocabulary size.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For the byte pair encoding (BPE), we instead train the tokenizer on raw text to automatically determine the vocabulary.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: In GPT2, they first split the raw text into words and then do this byte pair encoding.

There are a few additional implementation details to consider for project 1.
\mathsf {encode} loops over all merges. Only loop over merges that matter.
Detect and preserve special tokens (e.g. <|endoftext|>)
Use pre-tokenization (GPT2 regex)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We start by discussing a few essential questions one hopes to answer when they set out to train a large model.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: How long to train a 70b parameter model on 15T tokens on 1024 H100s?

Mysteriously, we spawn from the ether (will be explained later) that total_flops = 6 * 70e9 * 15e12. Intuitively, we know that we need to perform some computation for each parameter for each token. However, the 6 is still an ominous mystery. Given the total flops, we then can just take the GPUs FLOP/s multiply by the number of seconds in the day and divide total_flops by this number i.e. days = total_flops / flops_per_day

In practice, we must also consider the model flop utilization (MFU). Basically, our algorithm cannot utilize the entirety of the maximum FLOP/s provided by the hardware due to things like memory bandwidth, etc. To account for this in the calculation, they just take flops_per_day / 2.
What is the largest model that you can train on 8 H100s using AdamW?

An H100 has 80gb of shared memory i.e. h100_bytes = 80e9. The calculation then proceeds as # parameters, gradients, optimizer state bytest_per_param = 4 + 4 + (4 + 4) num_parameters = (h100_bytes * 8) / bytes_per_parameter what we need each of parameters, gradients, optimizer state will become clear later.
In practice, we also need to account for activations, which will depend on both batch size and sequence length.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Tensor Memory: Tensors are stored as a floating point number. Memory is determined by (i) number of values in tensor and (ii) data type of each value.

float16: here, we decrease both the fraction and exponent bits. Importantly, there are only 5 exponent bits making the effective range (2^{-6}, 2^{6}). The dynamic range for small numbers is pretty bad. This can cause small numbers to "die" effectively killing these neurons for the remainder of learning due to backpropagation.
bfloat16: to fix the problems with float16, they instead keep 8 exponent bits and reduce the fraction bits to 7. This maintains the same dynamic range as float32 while still only using 16 bits.

fp8: Two variants
E4M3: has 4 exponent bits and 3 mantissa bits
E5M2: has 5 exponent bits and 2 mantissa bits Requires modern Hopper Architecture i.e. H100 to perform operations. Also supported by TPUs.

html:ol.html:li: float32 training works but requires lots of memory
Using lower precision data types allows you to train larger models due to lower memory requirements, but can cause instability
To optimize this tradeoff, use mixed-precision training.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In pytorch, tensors are contiguous blocks of memory, where their shape is controlled by strides. A stride basically just refers to how far you have to jump in physical memory to correspond to incrementing the logical index i.e. in a (2, 3) matrix each row requires jumping 3 spots in physical memory. Thus, stride[1] = 3.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Calling .view((m, n)) just changes the stride but does not modify the physical memory. If you call .view that results in a non-contiguous view, then you cannot call .view again because it assumes contiguous indexing to properly adjust the strides. To deal with this, they have .contiguous() and .reshape() is basically .contiguous().view().
All elementwise operations make a copy i.e. x + y. A useful function for masked attention is triu().

Einops is a useful python library to keep track of high-dimensional matrix operations i.e. matmuls over batch_size, seqlen, etc. High level overview
Initialize like X[float.tensor, "batch seq hidden"] = torch.tensor((2, 3, 4))
Can perform matmul with named dims like z = einsum(x, y, "batch seq1 hidden, batch seq2 hidden -> batch seq1 seq2")
reduce: x = reduce(x, "x y z -> x y 1") is just like mean(x, axis=-1)
rearrange: x = rearrange(x, "... (heads hidden1) -> ... heads hidden 1")

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We refer to the total number of floating point operatings as FLOPS. Alternatively, we refer to the number of floating point operations per second as FLOP/s.
Given a promised FLOP/s, the model flop utilization is given by \frac {\text {actual FLOP/s}}{\text {promised FLOP/s}}

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Performing a matrix multiplication A \cdot B for A \in \mathbb {R}^{(B \times D)} and B \in \mathbb {R}^{(D \times K)}, requires 2 \times B \times D \times K FLOPS

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I hope to do a formal blog touring optimizers because I have written a paper on deep learning optimizers for my optimization course. Due to this, I will leave this blank for now. A brief list of the covered concepts are below:

html:ul.html:li: momentum
adagrad
RMSProp
Adam

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A key formula to remember: \text {total memory} \approx 4 \times (\text {params} + \text {activations} + \text {grad size} + \text {optimizer state size})

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Another useful trick is to use low precision for forward pass activations, then use float32 for gradients and network parameters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Pre-norm vs. Post-norm: The first architecture variation discussed is when to apply the layer norm. As can be seen in the figure below, rather than apply the layer norm in the residual stream. They instead place it before the FFN and MHA layers.
They actually found that adding layer normalization before and after the MHA and FFN works the best. This is known as double norm.
RMSNorm vs. LayerNorm: Another useful trick is to use the RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm.

The RMS norm is simply the layer norm without the added bias term and centering i.e. \mathrm {RMSNorm}: \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \frac {\mathbf {x}}{\sqrt {\mathrm {Var}[\mathbf {x}_i] + \epsilon }}*\gamma . This is used to spare memory movement of layernorm, where the bias term \beta \in \mathbb {R}^d and has been found to be empirically almost if not as good.
One might wonder if matrix multiplies are the only thing that matters what can such a small change really accomplish.

html:ul.html:li: Due to memory movement, despite being .17% of FLOPS, provided 25% speedup in runtime!!

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Most people apparently just drop the bias term and keep the centering i.e. subtracting the mean. This makes sense because computing the mean does not require loading any additional information back to memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Despite a long list of activations, the two focused on are ReLU and GELU.

The rectified linear unit(ReLU) is defined as \mathrm {ReLU} : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \max (0, \mathbf {x}), where the \max is done elementwise i.e. \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x})_i = \max (0, x_i) This provides "nice" gradients, making it common when training neural networks.

The Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) is a slight modification to the ReLU to account for the non-differentiability at 0. Namely, \mathrm {GELU}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} \cdot \psi (\mathbf {x}), where \psi (\mathbf {x}) = \mathrm {CDF}(\mathcal {N}(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {I}))

html:figure.html:figcaption: GELU vs. ReLU activation and derivative. Taken from here.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Swish activation is given by \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} s(\mathbf {x}), where s : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d is the sigmoid function.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The extra computation required by GELU or Swish is a non-factor because FLOPS are dominated by matrix multiplication and there is no increase in memory pressure.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A gated linear unit is an activation function combined with an elementwise multiplication i.e.
\mathrm {ReGLU} = \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x},
\mathrm {SwiGLU} = \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x}, where \mathrm {ReLU} is the ReLU activation and \mathrm {Swish} is the Swish activation respectively.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The matrix \mathbf {V} introduces additional learnable parameters. Therefore, an architecture that uses gating typically reduces their other parameters by a factor of \frac {2}{3}.

Another small trick is to parallelize computation. Traditionally, one computes the \mathrm {MHA} output and then passes this to the \mathrm {FFN} i.e. \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x} + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})))). However, this requires waiting for the \mathrm {MHA} to complete before performing the \mathrm {FFN} computation. It has been found that performing these in parallel does not cause any severe degradation in performance. Explicitly, instead they do \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})) + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x}))

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Notation: For this section, our notion of an embedding is a function f that takes a token and a position i.e. f(x, i) is the embedding of token x, occurring at position i.

Idea: the inner product of two embeddings should only need relative positioning i.e. for any two \mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y} with positions i and j respectively, there should exist some g, such that \langle f(\mathbf {x}, i), f(\mathbf {y}, j) \rangle = g(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y}, i - j) Namely, this can be written as a function of just the relative positioning between the embeddings.
The simplest transformation that only preserves this relative information are rotations.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Rotating embeddings does not change relative position.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, rotations are only easily defined for \mathbb {R}^2. To get around this, they simply partition their input 2d slices and apply the rotation via \mathrm {ROT} = \begin {bmatrix} \cos m \theta _{1} & - \sin m \theta _{1} & & & \\ \sin m \theta _{1} & \cos m \theta _{1} & & & \\ & & \cos m \theta _{2} & -\sin m \theta _{2} & \\ & & \sin m \theta _{2} & \cos m \theta _{2} & \\ & & & & \ddots \end {bmatrix}

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The \theta _i's are fixed and chosen according to some schedule to capture different "frequencies".

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The embedding are used by appling them to the query and key matrices separately i.e. W_K = \mathrm {ROT}(W_K), \quad W_Q = \mathrm {ROT}(W_Q) These are then what is used in self-attention.
Exception 1: in the case of GLUs, we recall that to account for extra parameters we scale \frac {2}{3}. Therefore, d_{\text {ff}} = \frac {8}{3}d_{\text {model}}.
Exception 2: In T5, they use d_{\text {ff}} = 64d_{\text {model}}. The logic for this was that we could maximize the MFU by increasing matrix size. Ended up going back to small d_{\text {model}}.

Rule 2: d_{\text {head}} = d_{\text {model}} / \text {num heads}
I believe the d_{\text {head}} is the output dim of each head.
This just says the total output dim for n heads is the same as if we did one head.
Means there is something important about splitting up the heads.

Rule 3: Aspect ratio = \frac {d_{\text {model}}}{n_{\text {layer}}} \approx 100-200
Pipeline dependent: if network speed is fast than parallelizing is easier and shallow networks are better.
If poor network speed then pipeline parallel might be more viable and deeper networks would be more parallelizable.

Rule 4: Vocab size
For single language models, vocab size is typically 30-50k
For multi language models, vocab size is typically 100-250k

Since there is so much data, it is not feasible to train your model for multiple epochs. This is actually nice in the sense that we do not have to worry about overfitting and performing regularization.

However, many of the large models are still trained with weight decay. Tatsu claims that this is not to do with regularization but actually due to some weird interaction with the learning rate schedule. I am not sure I entirely understood this part.

The softmax is ill-behaved due to the exponentials. In the transformer, we have two softmaxes: one at the end and one in the attention.
For the first, note \log \sigma (\mathbf {x})_i = \log (x_i) - \log \underbrace {\sum _{j=1}^{d} e^{x_j}}_{D(\mathbf {x})} The only problem is the denominator term. The idea is to enforce the D(\mathbf {x}) = 1 by regularizing via a penalty on \log D(\mathbf {x}) ie. \mathcal {L}_{\text {aux}} = 10^{-4} \log ^2(D(\mathbf {x})) With the \nabla D(\mathbf {x}) should be 0 and we are effectively only considering the non-exponential term.
For the attention, they primarily operate on the logits prior to the softmax. One way is via layer norm before the softmax. Another is via softcapping i.e. \mathrm {logits} = \mathrm {softcap} \cdot \tanh \left ( \frac {\mathrm {logits}}{\mathrm {softcap}} \right )
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Part of my revisiting CS fundamentals journey
The process abstraction, the syscalls for working with processes on Unix, the hardware-software contract that makes time-sharing safe, and the scheduling policies that decide who runs when.
A running program, plus all the state the OS needs to pause it and resume it later. A process consists of:

html:ol.html:li: Memory: an Address space holding the program's code, stack, and heap.
Registers: general-purpose registers carrying current computation, plus special-purpose registers:

html:ul.html:li: Program counter (PC): the next instruction to run.
Stack pointer and frame pointer: where the process sits on its stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A process moves between three states:

html:ul.html:li: running: currently on a CPU.
ready: runnable, waiting for the Scheduling to give it a CPU.
blocked: waiting on I/O or another event, not runnable.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Threads (see Concurrency Intro) share an address space within a process but each have their own PC, register set, and stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The minimum operations any OS must expose for working with Processes:

html:ol.html:li: Create: load a program from disk, set up its address space (stack, heap, code), and jump to main. The OS also initializes argc/argv.
Destroy: kill a misbehaving or unwanted process.
Wait: block until another process completes — useful for ordering dependent work.
Miscellaneous control: e.g. suspend / resume.
Status: report on running processes.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The stack is allocated up front. The heap starts small and grows via malloc, which calls into the OS (brk/sbrk) when it runs out of headroom.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: On Unix, the concrete realization of this API uses fork(), wait(), and exec() — covered separately.

The kernel-side data structure holding all per-process state. There is one PCB per Process, threaded together into the process list. The PCB is what the OS reads and writes during a Context switch: the saved register set lives here, as does the process state, the open files, the parent pointer, and so on.
An example from xv6:

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Threads need an analogous thread control block; see Concurrency Intro.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Unix system call that duplicates the calling Process. After fork() returns, two processes exist where there was one. The child gets its own copy of:

html:ol.html:li: Address space.
General-purpose registers.
Program counter — the child returns from fork() at the same instruction the parent does.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two processes are distinguished by fork()'s return value:

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Which process is scheduled first after fork() is non-deterministic. Use wait() if you need ordering.
The copy is logical, not physical: real implementations use Copy-on-write so the duplicated address space only allocates fresh pages when one side writes to them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Blocks the calling Process until one of its children terminates. The OS marks the child's PCB ZOMBIE on exit, keeping enough state (exit status, resource usage) for the parent to retrieve via wait(); only when the parent collects it is the child fully reaped.
wait() returns the PID of the child that died. Variant waitpid(pid, ...) blocks on a specific child — useful when a parent has multiple children and only one is interesting.
Used to enforce ordering between parent and child code paths after fork(), or to know when an exec()'d program finishes.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Calling wait() with no children to wait on returns -1.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Re-initializes stack and heap.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An asynchronous notification delivered to a Process. A process sends a signal with kill(pid, signum) (the name is misleading — most signals don't kill); the recipient can install a handler with signal(signum, handler) to "catch" it, otherwise the default action runs (often termination).

Signals are the OS's mechanism for events the recipient didn't ask to be told about: SIGINT from Ctrl-C, SIGSEGV from a Page fault on an invalid page, SIGCHLD when a child exits, SIGTERM from an administrative kill.
Who can signal whom depends on the user owning the process — a user can signal their own processes; the superuser can signal any process.

The OS lets user code run directly on the CPU for speed, but with hardware-enforced limits that prevent a buggy or malicious program from compromising the rest of the system. Two questions motivate the mechanism:

html:li.html:em: How do we make sure a program doesn't do what we don't want it to do?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first question is answered by hardware User mode vs kernel mode enforced via the Trap table, with a controlled Syscall mechanism as the only path from one to the other. The second is answered by periodic timer interrupts that trap to the OS, which performs a Context switch and runs the scheduler.
The whole stack is bootstrapped during the Boot sequence, when the kernel installs the trap table and starts the timer before handing control to user space.

Two privilege levels supported by the CPU. User mode restricts the running process to a limited set of instructions and a limited view of memory — usually just its own address space. Kernel mode runs the OS with full access to all instructions and all of physical memory.

Switching between modes is mediated by hardware via a Trap table populated at boot. The OS sets up the table; the CPU enforces it. A process cannot promote itself to kernel mode arbitrarily — it must invoke a Syscall mechanism, which traps to a fixed entry point in the OS.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Kernel mode is dangerous because the address space is effectively the entire machine. The mode bit + the trap table together are what makes it safe to time-share a CPU between mutually distrusting programs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A hardware-readable table installed by the OS at boot that maps each kind of exceptional control-flow event (syscall, interrupt, page fault, illegal instruction, ...) to the address of an OS handler. When the CPU takes a trap, it consults this table to decide where to jump, switches into kernel mode, and runs the handler.

The table is what makes it safe to expose privileged operations to user mode: user code never gets to pick the destination of a mode-switch, only the trap number. The OS controls every entry point, the CPU enforces the indirection.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Installing the trap table is itself a privileged operation. It happens once, early in boot, before any user process exists.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A syscall is a user-mode-initiated request for a privileged service from the kernel. Because user code cannot jump into kernel code directly, the mechanism is built around a single trap instruction plus a pre-agreed register convention identifying which service to perform.
The end-to-end sequence:

html:ol.html:li: User code places the syscall number in a pre-defined register and invokes the trap instruction.
Hardware saves enough state to resume the process and switches to kernel mode.
Hardware jumps to the OS-installed handler via the Trap table.
The handler reads the syscall number from the agreed register and dispatches to the corresponding OS routine.
The OS validates the request, performs the work, and arranges return values per the calling convention.
The OS restores user state and returns the process to user mode.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Because the register convention is fixed by ABI, the user-mode side of a syscall is typically hand-written in assembly within libc.
Interrupts work via a similar trap-handler mechanism, but are initiated by hardware (timer, device) rather than user code.
The kernel must validate any pointers or arguments the user supplies — user code cannot be trusted not to ask the kernel to read kernel memory on its behalf.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The operation of saving the full execution state of one process (or thread) and restoring another's so the CPU can resume it as if it had never been interrupted. On a trap into the kernel, the hardware stashes the user-visible state (PC, condition codes, stack pointer) into a known location, and the OS saves the remaining general-purpose registers as part of the switching code.
Two flavors of state are involved:

html:ol.html:li: User registers: the current program's context (general-purpose registers, stack vars).
Kernel registers: meta-information used to resume the trap correctly (PC at trap, syscall number, etc.).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Context switches are not free — they incur the cost of cache and TLB pollution in addition to the direct cost of saving/restoring state. This is the cost amortized by larger scheduling quanta and a recurring concern for any Concurrency system.
The fixed series of steps a machine performs from power-on until a usable operating system is running. Each stage runs from a known location and hands off control to the next.

html:ol.html:li: Power signal resets the CPU to a known state.
The CPU fetches from a hard-wired address — the BIOS (or UEFI firmware). The BIOS runs basic hardware checks (POST).
The BIOS loads and jumps to the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from a known location on disk.
The bootloader places the kernel image at the correct address and jumps to its entry point.
The kernel performs early setup: installs the Trap table (a special privileged instruction), starts the timer interrupt, sets up memory management, and finally enters user space.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The hardware is hard-wired only to find the BIOS. Everything past that is software the user can replace, which is why dual-booting and custom bootloaders work.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The scheduler decides which runnable Process to put on the CPU at each moment. There is no single right answer because the metrics conflict:

html:ol.html:li: Turnaround time: time from arrival to completion, T_{\text {completion}} - T_{\text {arrival}}. Minimized by running short jobs first.
Response time: time from arrival to first scheduling. Minimized by frequent rotation.
Fairness: every job makes progress; nobody starves.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First-in, first-out. Jobs are run to completion in the order they arrive. Trivially fair under any reasonable notion of fairness, but suffers from the convoy effect: a single long-running job at the head delays every shorter job behind it, inflating average turnaround time.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If the first arriving job is much longer than the rest, all subsequent jobs wait at least its full duration before getting any CPU.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shortest job first. Run the job with the smallest total work first, run-to-completion. If all jobs arrive at the same time, SJF provably minimizes average turnaround time.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Real workloads don't all arrive at t = 0. A long job that arrives slightly before a short one still gets to monopolize the CPU until it finishes — SJF degenerates to FIFO scheduling under sequential arrivals.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Removing the run-to-completion constraint and allowing preemption gives STCF scheduling.

Shortest time-to-completion first. A preemptive refinement of SJF scheduling: whenever the scheduler is invoked, it picks the job with the least remaining work. A short job arriving mid-execution can preempt a long one.
STCF is provably optimal for minimizing average turnaround time over all preemptive schedulers.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Long jobs starve in response time. The lowest-priority work might not get a CPU slice for a very long time, which is unacceptable for interactive workloads.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Cycle through all runnable jobs, giving each a fixed time slice — the stride, time slice, or quantum length — before preempting and moving to the next. The best you can do at minimizing response time: every job sees the CPU within at most N quanta where N is the runnable-job count.
The quantum length is a tradeoff:

html:ul.html:li: Short quantum: better response time, more frequent context switches, worse cache locality.
Long quantum: amortizes context-switch cost, worse response time.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worst-case response time for N jobs at quantum S is S(N-1).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Round-robin minimizes response time but is poor for turnaround time relative to STCF scheduling. A scheduler that wants both has to combine ideas — see Multi-level feedback queue.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A practical scheduler that approximates STCF scheduling's turnaround properties and Round-robin scheduling's response properties without requiring foreknowledge of job lengths. Maintain multiple priority queues; within a queue, schedule round-robin. Never service a lower-priority queue while a higher-priority queue has a runnable job.
The priority of a job is not assigned by the user — it is learned from behavior:

html:ul.html:li: New jobs enter at the top priority.
Each priority level has an allotment — the total CPU time a job may consume before being demoted.
Once a job exhausts its allotment at a level, it drops to the next level.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Short jobs that finish quickly never leave the top queue (good turnaround). Interactive jobs that yield often stay at the top (good response time). CPU-bound jobs sink to the bottom and get scheduled when nothing better is runnable.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Gaming the allotment: a job can issue I/O just before its allotment expires to avoid demotion, since I/O time doesn't count.
Charge the job's allotment for total time at the priority level, including time spent blocked.
Starvation of low-priority queues if higher queues never empty.
Periodically reset all jobs to the top priority. Cheap and effective.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A proportional-share scheduler. Each runnable job holds some number of tickets, drawn from a fixed total. On each scheduling decision, the scheduler picks a random ticket and runs the job that owns it. Over time, a job receives CPU time proportional to its share of the tickets.
The implementation is trivial: one random number per decision, plus a sum table. No global state about historical CPU usage is needed.
Mechanisms layered on top:

html:ul.html:li: Ticket currency — give each user a fixed number of tickets to split among their processes however they like.
Ticket transfer — a client process can hand tickets to a server to boost its priority while servicing the request.
Ticket inflation — a process can grow its share temporarily, with bounds set by trust.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The unsolved problem is how to assign tickets in the first place. The mechanism is simple; the policy is not.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A deterministic version of the same idea is Stride scheduling, which avoids the variance of random sampling at the cost of needing global state.

A deterministic proportional-share scheduler. Each job has a stride inversely proportional to its ticket count (the more tickets, the smaller the stride) and a running pass counter that accumulates its stride every time it's scheduled.
The decision is:

html:ol.html:li: Pick the job with the lowest pass.
Run it for one quantum.
Increment its pass by its stride.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A job with twice as many tickets has half the stride, so its pass grows half as fast, so it gets scheduled twice as often. Same long-run behavior as Lottery scheduling, but with zero variance.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: New jobs need an initial pass value. Setting it to 0 lets them dominate; setting it to the global minimum lets them merge in cleanly but requires tracking that minimum.
Each context switch is not free. Choosing a longer time slice amortizes that cost at the price of response time.
I/O-bound jobs are commonly handled by splitting a job into its non-I/O segments and scheduling them individually — turning one long-but-mostly-blocked job into many short, runnable ones.

html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads does SJF scheduling deliver the same turnaround as FIFO scheduling?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: For workloads that arrive in increasing order of total job time.

html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads and quantum lengths does SJF deliver the same response times as Round-robin scheduling?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: For shorter jobs and longer quantum lengths, RR and SJF should deliver similar response times.

html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with SJF as job lengths increase?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Response time degrades because the longest jobs have to wait for each of the shorter jobs to finish. If these shorter jobs become longer than the response time grows linearly.

html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with RR as quantum lengths increase? Can you write an equation that gives the worst-case response time, given N jobs?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The response time is the average over stride length number of preceding jobs. The formula can be written as \mathrm {RT} = \frac {1}{N} \sum _{i = 1}^{N} (i - 1)S = \frac {S(N - 1) N}{N} = S(N - 1)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worked homework for Three Easy Pieces. One section per chapter; the conceptual material for each chapter lives in the corresponding concept pages — Processes Intro across Process, Process API, Process control block; Process Interlude across fork(), wait(), exec(), Unix signals.

html:ol.html:li:
Run process.py with the flags -l 5:100, 5:100. What should the CPU utilization be?
Kellen Kanarios2026512AnswerThe flags correspond to running two processes each with 5 instructions where each instruction has probability one of using the CPU. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu with this, we can conclude that the utilization is 100%. We will execute one instruction for the first process until it completes, then the five instructions of the next process. If both are waiting on IO, then I do not think this would be the case?
Kellen Kanarios2026512Solution Using the solution flags -cp, there is 100% utilization.

Run process.py with the flags -l 4:100, 1:0. How long does it take to complete both processes?
Kellen Kanarios2026512AnswerThe flags correspond to running one process with 4 instructions with each instruction having probability one of using the CPU and one process with one instruction that uses the IO. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 io io_done It will take 4 + \text {time of IO}. Since the process with 4 non-IO-dependent instructions will run each of those instructions and then the next process will begin which must wait for the entire duration of IO.
Kellen Kanarios2026512SolutionUsing the -cp flags it takes 11 timesteps to complete which is \approx 4 + \text {time of IO}.

Now switch the order of the processes: ./process-run.py -l 1:0,4:100. What happens now? Does switching the order mat- ter? Why?
Kellen Kanarios2026512Answer The output of the code is Process 0 io io_done
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu Depending on the time of IO (the -L flag), it should take \max (5, 1 + \text {time IO}). Switching the order does matter because we can run the CPU instructions while waiting for non-blocking IO. This motivates the idea of scheduling.
Kellen Kanarios2026512Solution It only takes 7 timesteps because we are able to perform the IO operations off the CPU as expected.

We'll now explore some of the other flags. One important flag is -S, which determines how the system reacts when a process issues an I/O. With the flag set to SWITCH_ON_END, the system will NOT switch to another process while one is doing I/O, instead waiting until the process is completely finished. What happens when you run the following two processes, one doing I/O and the other doing CPU work? (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH_ON_END)
Kellen Kanarios2026512Answer The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED READY 1 3 BLOCKED READY 1 4 BLOCKED READY 1 5 BLOCKED READY 1 6 BLOCKED READY 1 7* RUN:io_done READY 1 8 DONE RUN:cpu 1 9 DONE RUN:cpu 1 10 DONE RUN:cpu 1 11 DONE RUN:cpu 1 As expected, it runs the IO process, blocking the CPU process until it is completed.

Now, run the same processes, but with the switching behavior set to switch to another process whenever one is WAITING for I/O (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH ON IO). What happens now? Use -c and -p to confirm that you are right

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: I assume that at timestep 1 we will still RUN:io. However, we will then switch the PID: 1 for the next 4 timesteps until it is completed. After one additional BLOCKED timestep, we will run RUN:io_done and complete both processes.
The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 3 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 4 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 5 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 6 BLOCKED DONE 1 7* RUN:io_done DONE 1

html:ol.html:li:
One other important behavior is what to do when an I/O completes. With -I IO RUN LATER, when an I/O completes, the process that issued it is not necessarily run right away; rather, whatever was running at the time keeps running. What happens when you run this combination of processes? (./process-run.py -l 3:0,5:100,5:100,5:100 -S SWITCH ON IO -I IO RUN LATER -c -p) Are system resources being effectively utilized?
Kellen Kanarios2026512Answer The resources are not efficiently utilized because we end up waiting until all of our CPU-bound processes finish before returning to the IO-bound process, which calls another IO:run and we do not get any concurrency benefit.
Kellen Kanarios2026512Solution The code output is pretty long but the end output is Stats: Total Time 31 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (67.74%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (48.39%)

Now run the same processes, but with -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE set, which immediately runs the process that issued the I/O. How does this behavior differ? Why might running a process that just completed an I/O again be a good idea?
Kellen Kanarios2026512Answer The behavior differs by returning to the IO-bound process, which can then immediately call the next IO:run before returning access to the CPU-bound processes, improving concurrency. In an attempt to predict the next IO call, it might be a good idea to run the process that just completed IO because it is more likely to make another IO call.
Kellen Kanarios2026512Solution The corresponding statistics are Stats: Total Time 21 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (100.00%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (71.43%)

Now run with some randomly generated processes, e.g., -s 1 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 2 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 3 -l 3:50,3:50. See if you can predict how the trace will turn out. What happens when you use -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE vs. -I IO RUN LATER? What hap- pens when you use -S SWITCH ON IO vs. -S SWITCH ON END?
Kellen Kanarios2026512Answer Since each instruction has a 50% chance of being CPU or I/O, I do not think there will be much benefit to IO_RUN_LATER vs. IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE. However, I believe it will be crucial to enable -S SWITCH_ON_IO over -S SWITCH_ON_END as the number of processes grow because we should always have waiting CPU instructions to run. In the case of two processes, we still may not improve that much because they will likely both be IO-bound at some point.
Kellen Kanarios2026512Solution For -s 1 (aka the seed), the observed performance between IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE and IO_RUN_LATER is the same. However, we do observe an improvement on both IO and CPU usage when we enable SWITCH_ON_IO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberate-Practice (6/66).

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork(). Before calling fork(), have the main process access a variable (e.g., x) and set its value to something (e.g., 100). What value is the variable in the child process? What happens to the variable when both the child and parent change the value of x

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Both the parent and the child get a copy of the variable values i.e. x. When they modify x it only affects the copy of x in their local address space.

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that opens a file (with the open() system call) and then calls fork() to create a new process. Can both the child and parent access the file descriptor returned by open()? What happens when they are writing to the file concurrently, i.e., at the same time?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Both do have access to the same file descriptor. I believe that they can interleave writes if writing at the same time but for me whichever process got ran first always finished writing prior the other starting. This may be a scheduling thing, where one process is able to complete before the other is scheduled?

html:ol.html:li: Write another program using fork(). The child process should print "hello"; the parent process should print "goodbye". You should try to ensure that the child process always prints first; can you do this without calling wait() in the parent?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Using the previous problem, in the child, we can write something to a file. Then, in the parent, we can enforce an ordering by making the parent wait in a while loop until it reads the value written by the child. However, this is terribly inconvenient (and probably why wait() exists).

html:ol.html:li: Now write a program that uses wait() to wait for the child process to finish in the parent. What does wait() return? What happens if you use wait() in the child?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: wait() returns the pid of the child process that died. Using wait() in the child just immediately returns -1.

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork() and then calls some form of exec() to run the program /bin/ls. See if you can try all of the variants of exec(), including execl(), execle(), execlp(), execv(), execvp(), and execvP(). Why do you think there are so many variants of the same basic call?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: It seems that some of them support different types of arguments i.e. execv family allows variable size argument list. Additionally, there is a distinction in the environment. This seems to be a list of environment variable values that exec{l,v}e() will search over. The p family use the same search as the shell i.e. the PATH.

html:ol.html:li: Write a slight modification of the previous program, this time using waitpid() instead of wait(). When would waitpid() be useful?
