fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If a parent process has multiple children, then wait() will only wait until the first one dies. waitpid() lets you specify waiting on a specific child process.

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates a child process, and then in the child closes standard output (STDOUT_FILENO). What happens if the child calls printf() to print some output after closing the descriptor?

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Nothing prints in the child process. However, stdout is still open in the parent process and able to print.

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates two children, and connects the standard output of one to the standard input of the other, using the pipe() system call

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: This required fflush() for me. I am not sure if this is true in general?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The address-space abstraction, the translation schemes that implement it, the algorithms that manage free space within a process, and what happens when physical memory is not enough.

We are now moving on from scheduling into the wonderful world of all things memory. The starting point is the address space abstraction — each process is handed its own private virtual memory and the OS, with hardware help, manages the mapping from virtual to physical addresses.
The progression of translation schemes covered in TPE:

html:ol.html:li: Base and bounds — one base+bound pair per process. Simple but wasteful.
Segmentation — per-region base+bound. Removes internal fragmentation, introduces external fragmentation.
Paging — fixed-size translation units. Eliminates external fragmentation at the cost of a translation table.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The chunk of memory the OS gives a process to use at its own discretion. Each process has its own address space, so that one process cannot read or overwrite another's program state. An address space typically contains three regions:

html:ol.html:li: Code: the program text.
Stack: local variables, function arguments, return values.
Heap: dynamically allocated memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every address a process sees is virtual — not the location in physical memory. The hardware translates virtual addresses to physical ones at access time. Approaches to that translation include Base and bounds, Segmentation, and Paging.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The API the user sees is tiny: a way to get memory and a way to release it, exposed as malloc and free. The common bugs are forgetting to initialize, forgetting to free, freeing too early (dangling pointer), freeing twice (double free), and freeing memory not from malloc (invalid free).
malloc and free are library calls, not syscalls. The heap is pre-allocated; malloc extends it by invoking brk/sbrk when it runs out.
When a process dies, the OS reclaims its memory, so a bounded leak is not catastrophic — just bad practice.
You can also acquire an anonymous heap-like region directly via mmap.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest hardware mechanism for Address space translation. The CPU has two dedicated registers per process — a base pointing to the start of the process's region in physical memory and a bound marking the end. Translation is just phys = base + virt, with a check that the result stays below bound. The unit performing this translation in hardware is the memory management unit (MMU).
The hardware must expose privileged instructions for the OS to set base and bound on a context switch, and trap on out-of-bound accesses.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: To support a large address space, base-and-bounds suffers significant internal fragmentation — the unused gap between the stack and heap is allocated but wasted. With 16KB total memory and 4KB-but-mostly-empty processes, you can run 4 processes when you'd really have room for 8.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Segmentation is the natural next step — give each region (code, stack, heap) its own base and bound so the gaps don't have to be allocated.

An Address space-translation scheme that gives each logical region of a process (code, heap, stack, ...) its own base and bound. Instead of allocating a single contiguous chunk with empty space between regions, the OS allocates each segment separately and on demand. A virtual address is split into a segment number and an offset within that segment:

The hardware uses the segment number to index a segment table, retrieves that segment's base and bound, and adds the offset. Per-segment protection bits make read-only code and no-execute data straightforward.
Segmentation also enables memory sharing — two processes can map the same physical segment (e.g. shared code) into both their address spaces.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The stack grows downward, so the bounds check has to be direction-aware based on the segment number.

Segmentation does not solve fragmentation; it pushes it from internal (per-process) to external (per-system). Variable-sized segments leave variably-sized holes in physical memory. The fix that eventually won is Paging.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Instead of allocating memory in variable-sized chunks (which fragments badly), paging divides virtual and physical memory into fixed-size pages and maps virtual pages to physical pages independently. This is the address-translation scheme that won.
The mechanism rests on three concepts:

html:ol.html:li: The Page table — per-process map from virtual page number to physical page number.
The Translation lookaside buffer — small hardware cache that absorbs the cost of consulting the page table on every access.
The Multi-level page table — the page table is itself paged, so processes only pay for the parts of the address space they use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a process touches a virtual address whose page isn't in physical memory, the page table's present bit is unset; the hardware traps to the OS as a page fault, and the OS retrieves the page from swap. The eviction policies that decide what to swap out are covered by Page replacement.

The two-level decomposition did not arrive fully formed. The Segmented page tables design tried to give each named segment of the address space its own variable-sized page table; the fragmentation that produced is what motivated the move to fixed-size paged page tables.

The per-process data structure that maps each virtual page number (VPN) to a physical page number (PPN). A virtual address is split into a VPN and an offset within the page; the page table translates the VPN, the offset is appended unchanged.
Each page-table entry (PTE) carries more than just a PPN:

html:ol.html:li: Valid bit: whether this VPN has memory allocated. Gaps between stack and heap need not be allocated.
Protection bits: read/write/execute permissions.
Present bit: whether the page currently resides in physical memory (vs. swap).
Dirty bit: has the page been modified since being paged in.
Reference bit: has the page been accessed (used by eviction policy).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The table gets very large. For a 32-bit address space with 4KB pages and 4-byte PTEs, a linear page table costs 2^{32}/2^{12} \cdot 4 = 2^{22} bytes — 4MB per process.
Translation requires accessing the table in memory, which is slow on every memory reference. Mitigated by the Translation lookaside buffer.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The size problem is mitigated by Multi-level page table, which only allocates the parts of the table that are actually used.
A small hardware cache of recent virtual-to-physical translations. Without it, every memory reference would incur the cost of walking the Page table in memory.
An access proceeds as:

html:ol.html:li: Extract VPN from the virtual address.
Check whether the VPN is in the TLB.
TLB hit: read PPN from the entry, append offset, access memory.
TLB miss: walk the page table (in hardware on CISC, by trapping to OS on RISC), insert the translation into the TLB, retry.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A TLB entry holds the VPN, the PPN, a valid bit (separate from the PTE valid bit — invalid on init, eviction, context switch), and protection bits.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Each process has different translations for the same VPN, so a naive TLB must be flushed on every context switch.
Add an address space identifier (ASID) to each TLB entry and an ASID register set on context switch. Now entries from different processes coexist.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: "Ram is not always ram". Referring to the fact that a physical memory access takes a substantial different amount of time depending on whether it hit in the TLB.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: In real software-managed TLBs, an entry has global bits for shared pages, an 8-bit ASID, coherence bits, and a wired register reserving slots for code that cannot afford a TLB miss (e.g. the TLB-miss handler itself).
For very fast caches, even a TLB access is a bottleneck. Virtually indexed caches sidestep the TLB on the index path.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An early translation scheme that splits the virtual address space into a small number of named segments (code, data, stack, heap, ...) and gives each segment its own Page table sized only to the range of addresses that segment actually uses. The virtual address is interpreted as a triple: segment number, page number within the segment, and offset within the page.

Translation is a two-step walk. The segment number indexes a segment table, which yields the base and length of that segment's page table. The page number then indexes that page table to find the physical frame, and the offset is appended. Empty or unused segments need no page table at all, so a process pays for memory it actually uses — the same motivation that drives every non-flat translation scheme.

The mechanism has appealing side benefits. Each segment carries its own protection bits, so the architecture can enforce no-execute on the stack or read-only on the code segment at the segment-table level without per-page complexity. Translation overhead is bounded by the segment count, which is typically very small.

The problems are what killed it. Page tables are now variable-sized objects that still have to live in contiguous physical memory. The stack segment's page table might be small; the heap's might be large and grow over time. Allocating, growing, and freeing variably-sized tables in kernel memory produces external fragmentation — exactly the headache pure segmentation has at the user level, now pushed down a layer. If the heap outgrows its current page table, the kernel has to allocate a larger contiguous chunk and copy.

Secondary issues compound. Translation costs two memory references (segment table, then page table) before reaching data, which hurts without a Translation lookaside buffer. The segment count is fixed by the architecture, which is awkward for programs whose layout does not fit "code/data/stack/heap" — a sprawling memory-mapped region or a JIT wanting many small protection domains has nowhere to go. And segmentation tends to leak into the programming model: x86 carried segment registers around for decades after they had stopped being useful.

The fix that won is the Multi-level page table — page the page table itself, in fixed-size chunks, and recover the "only pay for what you use" property without variable-sized contiguous allocations. Every modern architecture descends from that decision.

A page table that is itself paged: instead of one linear array of PTEs, the table is split into page-sized chunks indexed by a top-level page directory. Subtrees of the address space that are entirely unused get a single null entry at the top level — no second-level page is allocated for them. This is the design that displaced Segmented page tables — it preserves the "don't pay for empty regions" property while keeping every piece of the structure page-sized, eliminating the variable-allocation fragmentation that doomed the segmented version.
Advantages over a linear Page table:

html:ol.html:li: Fits easily in memory — only allocate a second-level page when an entry in it is used.
Memory cost is proportional to the amount of address space actually in use.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: On a Translation lookaside buffer miss, the walk now costs two memory loads — page directory entry first, then the PTE.
Real architectures push this further (x86-64 uses 4 or 5 levels) and lean on the TLB to absorb the walk cost.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once swap space is in play, the OS must decide which pages to evict from physical memory. The mechanism is the Page fault machinery; the question of policy is what this hub covers.

Physical memory is finite, so the OS reserves a region of disk — swap space — to hold pages that don't fit. Pages currently on disk have their present bit cleared in the Page table entry; the PTE instead holds a disk address.
When a process touches a virtual address whose page is not present, the hardware traps to the OS. This is a page fault. The handler:

html:ol.html:li: Looks at the PTE: if invalid, the access is illegal — terminate the process.
If valid but not present: read the page in from swap, update the PTE, retry the access.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The hardware never handles page faults itself — that would require it to understand disk I/O and the OS's swap layout. Page-fault handling is always in software.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The three cases on every memory access:

html:ol.html:li: Present + valid: hardware reads the PPN from the PTE and proceeds.
Not present + valid: hardware invokes the page-fault handler to retrieve from swap.
Not valid: hardware raises an exception; the OS typically kills the process.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: What happens when you dereference a nullptr? The TLB misses on VPN 0, the page-table walk finds the PTE marked invalid, and the hardware traps to the OS — which delivers SIGSEGV.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Least recently used is the standard heuristic for picking a page to evict: throw out the one that has been untouched the longest. It works because of the principle of locality — recently-used pages are likely to be used again soon (temporal locality).
Pure LRU requires updating a data structure on every memory access, which is too expensive. The clock algorithm approximates it cheaply:

html:ol.html:li: Maintain a reference bit per page, set by hardware on access.
Loop a pointer around the pages. If the reference bit is 1, clear it and advance.
If the reference bit is 0, evict that page.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: LRU is not optimal. The pathological case is the looping workload: if the working set is one page larger than the cache, LRU misses 100% of the time.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The quality of any replacement policy is measured by its average memory access time:
where T_P is physical-memory access time and T_D is disk access time. The disk gap is so large that even tiny miss rates dominate AMAT.
A desirable property of a page-replacement policy is the stack property: increasing the cache size never makes the miss rate worse. Surprisingly, not every reasonable-looking policy has it.

FIFO eviction violates the stack property — on certain access sequences, growing the cache strictly increases the number of misses. This is Belady's anomaly. It is what motivates LRU and the clock algorithm-style policies, which do have the stack property because they only depend on relative access recency.

The optimal eviction policy (offline) is to evict the page whose next use is farthest in the future. Unimplementable in practice (you don't know the future), but useful as a theoretical upper bound to compare against.

Pure LRU and the clock algorithm is a global policy — it does not distinguish memory by owner, so a single greedy process can hog memory. Segmented FIFO maintains a per-process FIFO queue capped at the process's resident set size; evicted pages go to one of two secondary lists:

html:ol.html:li: Clean page free list — evicted pages that haven't been modified.
Dirty page list — evicted pages that need to be written back.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If the original process faults on a page still on the dirty list, it can be reclaimed without I/O.

An optimization where two address spaces share the same physical page until one writes to it. Both processes have PTEs pointing at the same PPN, but with the write bit cleared. The first write traps to the OS, which allocates a fresh page, copies the contents, points the writing process's PTE at the new page (with writes re-enabled), and resumes.
Used pervasively:

html:ul.html:li: fork() — the child shares the parent's pages until either modifies one. Avoids the cost of copying an entire address space the child often immediately overwrites with exec().
Shared libraries — many processes map the same library text pages.
Kernel mapped into every process — same physical kernel code seen from every address space.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a process asks for new heap memory, the OS must guarantee the returned pages contain zeros (to avoid leaking other processes' data). The naive implementation zeroes the page at allocation time. On-demand zeroing defers that work to first touch: until the process actually accesses a freshly-allocated page, no zero-fill happens.

The OS marks the page as not-present in the Page table. The first access traps as a Page fault, the handler zeroes a physical page, installs it in the PTE, and resumes. Heap memory that is allocated but never used costs nothing.
Small page-out I/Os are amortized by batching: instead of writing one dirty page at a time, the OS collects a batch from the global dirty list and writes them together.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: A common convention is to map the kernel into every process's address space (via Copy-on-write) so kernel code and structures appear at the same virtual addresses everywhere.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Once we have an Address space, we still have to actually allocate physical bytes within it. Free-space management is the discipline of tracking which bytes are free, picking which free chunk to satisfy a request, and merging adjacent freed chunks back together. Two fragmentation hazards to watch:

html:ol.html:li: External fragmentation: many small unallocated non-contiguous chunks.
Internal fragmentation: allocated chunks larger than what the requester uses.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Simplifying assumptions for the algorithms below: allocations are immovable once placed, total memory is fixed, and the user does not specify a size in free. The last assumption is handled by storing the size in a header preceding the returned pointer.
Given a free list of unallocated memory chunks and a request of size n, which chunk do you carve the allocation out of? Different answers trade off fragmentation against search time.

html:ol.html:li: Best fit: find the smallest chunk \geq n. Minimizes wasted space per allocation but tends to leave many small unusable fragments behind, and requires scanning the whole list.
Worst fit: find the largest chunk. Aims to leave usefully-sized leftovers but also requires a full scan.
First fit: take the first chunk that fits. Fast (early exit) but biases small fragments toward the start of the list.
Next fit: like first fit, but resume the search from where the previous allocation ended. Spreads small fragments more evenly.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: On free, the allocator searches the list for adjacent free chunks and coalesces them into a larger chunk. The size of each allocation is stored in a header immediately preceding the returned pointer so free doesn't need a size argument.

A data-structure-level optimization for allocators that maintains separate free lists per common object size. Instead of one global free list scanned by Malloc placement policies, you keep a list per size class. Allocations of that size pop straight off the matching list — no search.

html:ul.html:li: Grow a per-size list by requesting larger segments from the main allocator and chopping them up.
Drain back to the main list when no references remain.
Optionally leave objects pre-initialized on the list to skip constructor/destructor calls.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: This is the structure behind kernel slab allocators and many object-pool patterns. It works when the workload allocates many objects of a few common sizes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An allocator for memory of total size 2^n that maintains a binary-tree of free blocks at every power-of-two size. To service a request, walk down to the smallest block \geq requested size; if no block of that size exists, split a larger one (and its "buddy" becomes free). On free, check whether the freed block's buddy is also free, and if so, coalesce upward — recursively.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Memory of size 2^n organized as a buddy system.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key trick is that buddies differ by exactly one bit in their address at each level. To check whether a block's buddy is free, flip that bit and consult a bitmap. To coalesce upward, mask off the bit and recurse.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Internal fragmentation is bounded by 2× the request size (you may have to round up to the next power of two), but external fragmentation is essentially eliminated within the buddy region.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Threads, the hazards that come with sharing state between them, and the primitives that make sharing safe.

Concurrency comes from sharing: multiple Threads within a single process share the address space, which is what makes them cheap to spawn and to communicate between, and is also what makes them dangerous. When threads interleave on a shared variable, you get a Race condition. The fragile region is the Critical section, protected by synchronization primitives like Locks and Barriers.

An independent unit of execution within a Process. Each thread has its own program counter, register set, and stack — but shares the surrounding address space, file descriptors, and other process-level resources with its siblings.

To support a context switch between threads, the OS keeps a thread control block (TCB) per thread — the per-thread analog of Process control block. Each thread also gets its own stack within the process's address space.
Why threads instead of processes:

html:ol.html:li: Intra-process parallelism: parallelizable work within one program, without paying the context switch cost of separate processes.
Avoiding blocking I/O: one thread blocks waiting on I/O while others keep running.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Sharing an address space is exactly what makes threads cheap to communicate between and exactly what makes them dangerous — see Race condition and Critical section.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When the correctness of a program depends on the order in which concurrent Threads schedule, you have a race condition. The classic example: two threads each execute
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: mov addr reg // load add 1 reg // increment mov reg addr // store
fr:mainmatter.html:p: intending to increment a shared variable twice. If the OS interleaves the threads at the wrong instruction boundary:

html:ol.html:li: Thread 1 stores its incremented value.
Thread 2 stores its incremented value.
Thread 1 increments (in its register).
Thread 2 increments (in its register).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The result is a single increment, not two — both threads loaded the same starting value and the second store clobbered the first.
The fix is to prevent interleaving inside the code region that touches shared state — the Critical section — using a synchronization primitive like Locks.

A region of code that accesses shared state in a way that is incorrect if more than one Thread executes it concurrently. Critical sections are exactly the regions that need protection by a synchronization primitive: a Locks excludes other threads while one is inside; a Barriers forces all threads to reach the same point before any may continue.
The art of writing correct concurrent code is to identify critical sections and protect them — without making them so large that you lose all parallelism.

A lock is a synchronization primitive that grants mutually exclusive access to a critical section. The implementations below trade off coherence traffic, fairness, and memory in different ways. The naive locks (Test-and-set lock and Test-and-test-and-set lock) are simple but generate O(n) coherence traffic per release; queue-based locks (Ticket lock, Array-based lock, MCS lock) scale better at the cost of extra state per waiter.

The simplest spin lock. The acquire loop spins on an atomic test_and_set: it writes LOCKED and reads the previous value in one step. If the previous value was UNLOCKED, the caller has the lock; otherwise it tries again.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Major coherence traffic. Every spinning core must acquire write permissions and invalidate everyone else on each test_and_set, so contention scales badly with the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A refinement of the Test-and-set lock that reduces coherence traffic during the spin. Threads first perform a plain load to check whether the lock looks free, and only attempt the atomic test_and_set when it does. Spinning is then read-only, so cores can share the cache line.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Spinning is a read-only operation now.

When the lock is released, the line must be invalidated on every waiter, and they all simultaneously read and try to write. The contention is shifted from steady state to release time but is still O(n) in the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A fair spin lock based on two counters. Each arriving thread takes a "ticket" by atomically incrementing next_ticket and then spins until now_serving equals its ticket. Release simply increments now_serving. Threads acquire the lock in FIFO order, which prevents starvation but does not by itself reduce contention.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock_ptr): my_ticket = fetch_and_increment(lock_ptr->next_ticket) while (lock_ptr->now_serving != my_ticket); // spin
release(lock_ptr): lock_ptr->now_serving = lock_ptr->now_serving + 1

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Still O(n) coherence traffic on release: incrementing now_serving invalidates the line on every waiter, and they all reload it to recheck their ticket.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each waiter is assigned its own slot in an array and spins only on that slot. Release just flips the next slot to "go-ahead", so the cache line touched by release is the one read by exactly one other thread. Coherence traffic per release is constant rather than O(n), at the cost of one slot per waiter.

html:ul.html:li: Give each waiter its own "slot" to wait on.
Only need to set the next slot to "go-ahead" on release.
Constant coherence traffic.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: O(n) memory for the lock array, and padding each slot to 64b (one cache line) to avoid false sharing makes that cost real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A scalable, list-based queue lock. The lock variable is a pointer to the tail of a linked list of per-thread nodes. Each thread enqueues its own node and spins on a flag in its own cache line. The releaser wakes only its direct successor, so coherence traffic per handoff is constant and memory cost is proportional only to threads currently waiting, not to the lock's fanout.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock): I->next = null predecessor = fetch_and_store(lock, I) if predecessor != nil: // some node holds the lock I->must_wait = true predecessor->next = I // predecessor must wake us repeat while I->must_wait // spin till lock is free
release(lock): if (I->next == null): // no known successor if compare_and_swap(lock, I, nil): // make sure... return // CAS succeeded; lock freed repeat while I->next == nil // spin to learn successor I->next->must_wait = false // wake successor

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A barrier holds threads at a synchronization point until all P participants have arrived, then releases them simultaneously. The naive implementation uses a shared counter that each arriving thread decrements; the last arrival resets the counter and the rest spin until it returns to P.

fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
global (shared) count : integer := P
procedure central_barrier if fetch_and_decrement(&count) == 1 count := P else repeat until count == P

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: When count == P, a fast thread can reach the next barrier and decrement count again before the slow threads observe the reset. The slow threads then spin forever waiting for a value that has already passed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The fix is to give each thread a private local_sense that flips on each barrier. The global sense flag is flipped by the last arrival, and waiters spin on sense == local_sense, so a fresh barrier cannot be confused with a stale one.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
global (shared) count : integer := P global (shared) sense : bool := true global (private) local_sense : bool := false
procedure central_barrier local_sense = !local_sense if fetch_and_decrement(&count) == 1 count := P sense = local_sense else repeat until sense == local_sense

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We define the entropy of a random variable X \sim p_{X}, where p_{X} : \mathcal {X} \to [0, 1] as H(X) = -\sum _{x \in |\mathcal {X}|} p_{X}(x) \log p_{X}(x)
C is said to be convex if for any x_1, x_2 \in C and \lambda \in [0,1] then \lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda )x_2 \in C

A function f : C \to \mathbb {R} is said to be convex if C is convex, and for any x_1, x_2 \in C, \lambda \in [0,1], we have f(\lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda )x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1 - \lambda )f(x_2) and strictly convex if the inequality is strict.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: This can be thought of as the tangent is always an underestimate of a convex function.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If f: \mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R} is twice-differentiable, then f is convex if and only if \nabla ^2 f(\boldsymbol {x}) \succeq 0.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: This implies positive curvature or the common "bowl-shaped" interpretation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If \phi is a convex function on the range of X then \phi (\mathbb {E}[X]) \leq \mathbb {E}[\phi (X)]

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: When is D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P || Q) = 0?

Need Q(x) / P(x) to be constant almost surely. This occurs only when Q = P.
Q(x) / P(x) = c \implies Q(x) = P(x) c \iff \sum Q(x) = c \sum P(x) \implies c = 1

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If a_{i},b_{i}>0 for i\in \{1,\ldots ,n\}; A=\sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i},\quad B=\sum _{i=1}^{n}b_{i} Then, we have: \sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i}\log a_{i}/b_{i}\geq A\log A/B

html:ul.html:li: Joint convexity of D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P || Q),
Individual convexity of D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P || Q).
Concavity of entropy.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Information Theory › Data Processing Inequality

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Information Theory › Data Processing Inequality

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
High-level
Y contains more information about X than \hat {X}.
We cannot increase the amount of information about X by processing Y. Markov Chains:
(U, V, W) form a Markov chain if
\begin {align*} P(W = w \mid U = u, V = v) = P(w = w \mid V = v) \end {align*}
Denoted U \to V \to W.
Equivalently, we say (U \perp W) | V which means P(W = w, U=u \mid V = v) \implies P(W = w \mid V = v)P(U = u \mid V = v)
Reversibility: U \to V \to W \iff W \to V \to U.
Estimation: X \to Y \to \hat {X}
202529https://kellenkanarios.com/004Z/004Z/004Z/Data Processing InequalityTheorem
Suppose that U \to V \to W. Then I(U; W) \leq I(U; V).
Intuition: by data processing, we can never create new information about U.
202529Proof Use chain rule to expand I(U; (V, W)) in two ways i.e.
\begin {align*} I(U; (V, W)) = I(U; V) + I(U; W | V) = I(U; W) + I(U; V | W) \end {align*}
Since U \to V \to W is a Markov chain, I(U; W | V) = 0. Therefore,
\begin {align*} I(U; V) &= I(U; W) + I(U; V | W) \\ &\geq I(U; W) \end {align*}

html:ul.html:li: H(X | Y) controls the error probability P_e.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
\{X_{n}:n\geq 1\}: a sequence of i.i.d. r.v.s. with mean \mu , variance \sigma ^{2}. Then,
\frac {1}{n}\sum _{i=1}^{n}X_{i}\stackrel {{p}}{{\rightarrow }}\mu

\{X_{n}:n\geq 1\}: a sequence of i.i.d. r.v.s. with mean \mu , variance \sigma ^{2}. Let f:\mathcal {X}\rightarrow \mathbb {R} be any function, such that \mathrm {Var}(f(X))=\sigma _{f}^{2}<\infty . Then, \frac {1}{n} \sum _{i = 1}^{n} f(X_i) \stackrel {p}{\rightarrow } \mathbb {E}[f(X)]
Let X_{n}\stackrel {{p}}{{\rightarrow }}X, and f:\mathcal {X}\rightarrow \mathbb {R} denote a continuous function. Then, f(X_n) \stackrel {p}{\rightarrow } f(X)

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
We have the following important properties
\mathbb {P}(A_{\epsilon }^{(n)})\geq 1-\epsilon {\text { for large enough }}n
|A_{c}^{(n)}|\leq 2^{n(H(X)+\epsilon )}
|A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}|\geq (1-\epsilon )2^{n(H(X)-\epsilon )}\;\text {for large enough }\;n\;, Together, this says that, depending on the entropy, the typical set can contain most of the probability despite being much smaller than the entire space.

html:ul.html:li: Contains almost all the probability.
Consists of \approx 2^{nH(X)}
Almost equiprobable sequences.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let B_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\subset \mathcal {X}^{n} be the smallest set with probability at least 1-\epsilon . Then, we must have
\mathbb {P}(A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\cap B_{\epsilon }^{(n)})\geq 1-2\epsilon

html:ul.html:li: Rate = \frac {\log _2 \theta }{n}
Probability of decoding error = \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n|

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A positive real number R is said to be achievable if for all \epsilon > 0 (perturbation parameter) there exists a block length N(\epsilon ) such that \forall n > N(\epsilon ) the following holds
There exists an (n, \theta ) coding system such that \frac {\log \theta }{n} < R + \epsilon and \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n| < \epsilon .

Alternatively, a positive real number R is said to be achievable if there exists a sequence of coding systems (n, \theta ) such that \lim _{n \to \infty } \frac {\log \theta }{n} \to R and \lim _{n \to \infty } \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n| \to 0.
The minimum rate of source coding R_s is the infimum over all R such that R is achievable i.e. R_s \coloneqq \inf \{R \mid R \text { is achievable}\}

First we will show that H(X) is achievable. Then we will have R_s \leq H(X) by definition. Idea: take \theta = 2^{n(H(X) + \epsilon )} + 1. Give each element in typical set a unique index, everything else the same index. By properties of typical set, \mathbb {P}(\text {error}) < \epsilon for sufficiently large n.

A coding system (variable rate) with parameter n and an encoder-decoder pair (e,f), where e: \mathcal {X}^n \to \{0, 1\}^*, \quad \text {and} \quad f: \{0, 1\}^* \to \mathcal {X}^n We want the probability of error to be exactly zero; that is \mathbb {P}(X^n \neq f(e(X^n))) = 0.
Let \ell (x^n) denote the number of bits in e(x^n), where e is the encoding function. Then, the rate is \frac {\mathbb {E}[\ell (X^n)]}{n}

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Although it is unclear here, the difference between variable and fixed rate source coding is that we allow our encoder and decoder to map to/from \{0,1\}^* , where ^* is variable i.e. our encoded versions of our alphabet can have varying lengths.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If e is many-to-one then we say that e is singular. Similarly, if e is one-to-one then e is non-singular.

Given a code with n = 1, we define its extension e^*; that maps \mathcal {X}^* \to \{0,1\}^* by concatenation. That is e^*(x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots , x_m) = (e(x_{1}), e(x_{2}), \ldots , e(x_m)) A code with encoder e is said to be uniquely decodable if e^* is non-singular.
A code is said to be prefix-free or instantaneous if no codeword is a prefix of another.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Prefix free codes are uniquely decodable. To show unique-decodability, you simply have to provide a way to decode and show it is unique. For prefix-free, greedily decoding suffices.

Note that non-singular does not imply uniquely decodable. This is because for uniquely decodable we require the extension to be non-singular. For example, if we have that e(0) = 0, e(1) = 1, e(2) = 01. Then e^*(01) = e^*(2). From this, we can see that uniquely decodable is a much stronger condition.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let e: \mathcal {X} \to \{0,1\}^* denote a prefix-free code with lengths \ell _i = \ell (x_i) for x_i \in \mathcal {X}. Suppose |\mathcal {X}| = m. Then the following must be true: \sum _{i=1}^{m} 2^{-\ell _i} \leq 1

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Other direction is also true. For any \ell _i satisfying Kraft's inequality, one can construct a prefix-free code whose lengths of e(x_i) = \ell _i.
Surprisingly, any uniquely decodable code must also satisfy Kraft's inequality.

fr:title.text: Performance Limit of Variable Rate Source Codes

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Performance Limit of Variable Rate Source Codes

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A variable rate code with parameter n is a prefix-free code e : \mathcal {X}^n \to \{0, 1\}^* , and a decoder f : \{0,1\}^* \to \mathcal {X}^n

A nonnegative number R is said to be achievable if for all \epsilon > 0, there exists an n(\epsilon ) such that for all n \geq n(\epsilon ), the following holds
There exists a variable rate code with \mathbb {E}[\ell (X^n)]/n = R and \mathbb {P}(X^n \neq f(e(X^n))) = 0 for all n.
The minimum rate of variable rate source coding is R_s \coloneqq \inf \{R \mid R \text { is achievable}\}
Idea: map symbol x to interval in (0,1) using the CDF of X.

html:ul.html:li: Assume x_{1} < x_{2} < \cdots < x_m
X \sim P_X is \mathcal {X}-valued r.v. with \mathbf {p}_X
CDF of X = F_X(x) \coloneqq \sum _{x_i \leq x} P(x_i)
Midpoint function: G(x) = \sum _{x_i < x} P_X(x) + \frac {P_X(x)}{2} = F_X(x) - \frac {P_X(x)}{2}
Codeword: e(x) = \left \lfloor G(x) \right \rfloor _{\ell (x)}= first \ell (x) bits of G(x)

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Given x_1, \ldots x_m proceed as follows:
Take two x_i, x_j with smallest p(x_i), p(x_j) append 0 and 1 to their respective codes.
Combine them and treat them as a new value with p(x_i') = p(x_i) + p(x_j)
Repeat

Let X \sim P_X be an \mathcal {X}-valued random variable. Define L_H = \mathbb {E}_{X \sim P_X}[\ell _H(x)] and L^*(P_X) = \min _{\ell _1, \ldots , \ell _m} \sum _{i = 1}^{m} P_X(x_i) \ell _i. Then L_H = L^*(P_X).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: SFE satisfies L_H < H(X) + \frac {2}{n} i.e. it achieves the optimal rate as n \to \infty . Huffman coding achieves optimality for all finite values n.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
The "type" \widehat {P}_{x^{n}} associated with x^{n}\in \mathcal {X}^{n} is the probability distribution on \mathcal {X} with pmf \widehat {\boldsymbol {p}}_{x^{n}} , defined as
\widehat {\boldsymbol {p}}_{x^{n}}(a)=\frac {N(a|x^{n})}{n},\quad \text {where}\quad N(a|x^{n}):=\sum _{i=1}^{n}\mathbf {1}_{x_{i}=a}.

For any n\geq 1 , let \mathcal {P}_{n} denote the set of all possible types over the alphabet \mathcal {X} with denominator n . That is,
\mathcal {P}_{n}=\{\widehat {P}_{x^{n}}:x^{n}\in \mathcal {X}^{n}\}.

For any type P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , we can associate the type class T(P) , defined as
T(P)=\{x^{n}:\tilde {P}_{x^{n}}=P\}.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Let \mathcal {X} = \{1,2,3\}. For n = 2, x^n = 12 the type is (\frac {1}{2},\frac {1}{2}, 0) and the type class is \{12, 21\} .

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Suppose |\mathcal {X}|=m , and \mathcal {P}_{n} denotes the set of all distinct types that can be constructed using elements of \mathcal {X}^{n} . Then, we have_
|\mathcal {P}_{n}|=\binom {n+m-1}{m-1}\leq (n+1)^{m}.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: \mathcal {X}^n has exponentially many elements in n. However, among the exponentially many elements they can only create polynomially many types in n for fixed m.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} denote a type with denominator n . Then, the type class associated with P , defined as T(P)=\{x^{n}:\hat {P}_{x^n}=P\} satisfies
|T(P)|=\prod _{i=1}^{n}\binom {n-N_{i-1}}{N_{i}},\quad \text {where}\quad N_{0}=0,\text {and}\ N_{i}=n\times P(a_{i}),
for all a_{i}\in \mathcal {X}=\{a_{1},\ldots ,a_{m}\} .

Suppose X_{1},X_{2},\ldots ,X_{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q for an arbitrary distribution Q over the alphabet \mathcal {X} . Then, we have
\mathbb {P}(X^{n}=x^{n}) \equiv Q^{n}(x^{n}) = 2^{-n(H(\widehat {P}_{x^{n}})+D_{2n}(\widehat {P}_{x^{n}})=Q)}.
In other words, the probability that X^{n} takes a value x^{n} only depends on x^{n} through its type \widehat {P}_{x^{n}} . Furthermore, types that are far away from Q in relative entropy are exponentially less probable than those that are close to Q .
An immediate consequence of this is that if Q\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , and x^{n}\in T(Q) , then Q^{n}(x^{n})=2^{-nH(Q)} .

Let P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , and T(P) denote its type class. Then, the following bounds hold:
(n+1)^{-m}2^{nH(P)}\leq |T(P)|\leq 2^{nH(P)}.

fr:title.text: The probability of a type class

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The probability of a type class

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X^{n}=(X_{1},\ldots ,X_{n})\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q for an arbitrary distribution Q on \mathcal {X} . Then, we have the following:
(n+1)^{-m}2^{-nD_{KL}(P|Q)}\leq Q^{n}(T(P))\leq 2^{-nD_{KL}(P|Q)}.
In other words, the probability that X^{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q lies in the type class of P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} is \approx 2^{-nD_{KL}(P||Q)} .

Let X^{n}\sim Q^{n} (i.e., the X 's are drawn i.i.d. from Q ), and E\subset \Delta _{m} denote a subset of pmfs over the finite alphabet X=\{a_{1},\ldots ,a_{m}\} . Let Q^{n}(E) denote the probability \mathbb {P}_{X^{n}\sim Q^{n}}(\widehat {P}_{X^{n}}\in E) . Then, we have
Q^{n}(E)\leq 2^{-nD_{\mathbb {E}}(P^{n} \parallel Q)},
where P^{*}=\inf _{P^{n}\in E}D_{\mathbb {E}}(P^{*}\mid Q) is the closest element of E to Q in terms of relative entropy. Furthermore, if E is the closure of its interior, we also have
\operatorname *{lim}_{n\to \infty }-{\frac {1}{n}}\log Q^{n}(E)=D_{K L}(P^{*}\parallel Q).

A binary hypothesis testing problem involves three components: the data X\sim P , the null hypothesis class \mathcal {P}_{0} , and the alternative distribution class \mathcal {P}_{1} , and the goal is to use the data to decide between the null and alternative; that is,
\text {given}\ X\sim P,\quad \text {decide between}\quad H_{0}:P\in \mathcal {P}_{0},\quad \text {versus}\quad H_{1}:P\in \mathcal {P}_{1}.
When {\cal P}_{0} and {\cal P}_{1} are singleton sets: that is {\cal P}_{0}=\{P_{0}\} and {\cal P}_{1}=\{P_{1}\} with known P_{0},P_{1} , then the problem is called simple hypothesis testing, and this is what we will focus on mostly in this course:
\text {given}\ X\sim P,\quad \text {decide between} \quad H_{0}:P=P_{0},\quad \text {versus}\quad H_{1}:P=P_{1}.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Information Theory › Notions of optimality

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Information Theory › Notions of optimality

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given \alpha \in (0,1). The optimal hypothesis test in the Neyman-Pearson framework is one that satisfies \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z\mid X}}\;\mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}\left (Z=0\right ),\quad \mathrm {subject\,to}\quad \mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}\left (Z=1\right )\leq \alpha .

Assume we have prior distributions (\pi _{0}, \pi _{1}) over the two hypotheses and there exists costs C_{1|0}, C_{0|1} associated with type I and type II error respectively. Then the Bayes optimal solution is the solution of \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\pi _{0}C_{1|0}\mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}(Z=1)+\pi _{1}C_{0|1}\mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}(Z=0).

Find a test that minimzes the maximum of the two errors i.e. \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\operatorname *{max}\left \{\mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}(Z=1),\ \mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}(Z=0)\right \}=\operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\operatorname *{max}\left \{\pi _{1|0}(P_{Z|X}),\ \pi _{0|1}(P_{Z|X})\right \}.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Information Theory › Optimal NP Test

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Information Theory › Optimal NP Test

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X^{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}P_{X} , and consider a binary hypothesis testing problem with H_{0}:P_{X}=P_{0} versus H_{1}:P_{X}=P_{1}. Let (\pi _{0},\pi _{1}) denote the prior distribution of the two hypotheses being true. Then, the Bayes optimal hypothesis test with uniform loss is defined as
\Psi _{D}^{*}(X^{n})=\operatorname *{argmin}_{\Psi }\pi _{0}\pi _{10}(\Psi )+\pi _{1}\pi _{01}(\Psi )=\begin {cases}1&\text { if }L(X^{n})>\pi _{0}/\pi _{1}\\ 0&\text {otherwise.}\end {cases},

fr:title.text: Notebook: Information Theory › Error Exponents in Hypothesis Testing

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Information Theory › Error Exponents in Hypothesis Testing

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
In the previous section, we consider the optimal test in the non-asymptotic regime i.e. for finite n what test minimizes the error according to some notion of error. In this section, we ask the question of what is the rate that this error converges to 0. More specifically, if \text {error}_n \leq 2^{-nV} then what is the best (largest) V we can hope to achieve?
Kellen Kanarios202521Notebook: Information Theory › NP Error Exponent 2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006G/006G/006G/Optimal Error Exponent in NP settingDefinition
We say that an error exponent V is achievable with type-1 error \alpha >0 , if there exist a sequence of tests (\Psi _{n}) with errors (\alpha _{n},\beta _{n}) , satisfying
\alpha _{n}\leq \alpha ,\quad \text {and}\quad -\frac {1}{n}\log \beta _{n}\geq V.
Let V_{\alpha }:=\sup \{V:V\text { is achievable with type-1 error }\alpha \} . The optimal error exponent is defined as V^{*}=\inf \{V_{\alpha }:\alpha >0\}=\lim _{\alpha \downarrow 0}V_{\alpha } .
2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006H/006H/006H/Stein's LemmaTheorem
For any \alpha >0 , we have V_{\alpha }=D_{KL}(P_{0}\parallel P_{1}) , which also implies that V^{*}=D_{KL}(P_{0}\parallel P_{1}) . In other words, the relative entropy between P_{0} and P_{1} is the optimal type-II error exponent associated with a binary hypothesis testing problem with H_{0}:P_{X}=P_{0} and H_{1}:P_{X}=P_{1} .
Kellen Kanarios202521Notebook: Information Theory › Bayesian Error Exponent 2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006I/006I/006I/Optimal Bayesian Error ExponentDefinition
Consider a binary Bayesian hypothesis testing problem with prior distribution (\pi _{0},\pi _{1}) . Then, we say that an exponent V>0 is achievable, if there exist a sequence of tests \{\Psi _{n}:n\geq 1\} , with probability of errors \{p_{n}(\Psi _{n})=\pi _{0}\psi _{0}(\Psi _{n})+\pi _{1}\psi _{01}(\Psi _{n}):n\geq 1\} , such that
\liminf _{n\to \infty }\frac {1}{n}\log \left (\frac {1}{p_{n}}\right )=\liminf _{n\to \infty }\frac {1}{n}\log \left (\frac {1}{\pi _{0}\psi _{10}(\Psi _{n})+\pi _{1}\psi _{01}(\Psi _{n})}\right )\geq V.
The optimal Bayesian error exponent for binary hypothesis testing with distributions P_{0} and P_{1} is defined as V^{*}\equiv V^{*}(P_{0},P_{1})=\sup \{V>0:V\text {is achievable}\} .
2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006J/006J/006J/Optimal Bayesian Error ExponentTheorem
For any \lambda \in [0,1] let P_{\lambda } denote the distribution over \mathcal {X} such that P_{\lambda }(x)\propto P_{0}^{\lambda }(x)\times P_{1}^{\lambda -1}(x) . Let \lambda ^{*}\in [0,1] denote the value at which D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{0})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{1}) . Then, the optimal error exponent in the Bayesian hypothesis testing problem with distributions P_{0} and P_{1} satisfies:
V^{*}(P_{0},P_{1})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{0})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{1}).

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A sequence of fixed-rate source codes \{(e_{n},f_{n}):n \geq 1\} with parameters \{(n,2^{nR_{n}}):n \geq 1\} is said to be universal with rate R if the following are true:
The limiting rate satisfies \lim _{n\to \infty }R_{n}\leq R .
For any distribution P over the alphabet \mathcal {X} with H(P)<R , the probability of error, \mathbb {P}_{X^{n}\sim P^{n}} \left (f_{n}(e_{n}(X^{n})) \neq X^{n}\right )=0.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Information Theory › Construction of universal fixed rated code

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Information Theory › Construction of universal fixed rated code

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Suppose X\sim Q , but we encode it using an optimal code for P . Then, the redundancy incurred is approximately equal to D_{KL}(Q\parallel P) .

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: It will turn out the optimal choice of Q is the centroid associated with the k elements of \mathcal {P}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: \mathcal {R}^*(\mathcal {P}) = C(\mathcal {P}) \coloneqq \sup _{\pi \in \Delta _k} I(W; X), where I(W; X) is the mutual information between W \sim \mathbf {\pi } and the output X, with X | (W = i) \sim P_i. The optimal Q^* is Q_{\pi }^* = \sum _{i = 1}^{k} \pi _i^* P_i.

The algorithm proceed as follows:
Initalize the dictionary to an empty set
Make one pass through the stream of data
Add every new "phrase" to the dictionary
Encode only the elements of the dictionary

A code with parameter (M, n) i.e. (number of messages, number of channel users) consists of an encoder decoder pair
e : [M] \to \mathcal {X}^n
f: \mathcal {Y}^n \to [M]
The rate is \log _{2}\frac {M}{n} = (log of the number of distinct pairs)/(number of channel users)
P_{e, \text {max}} = \max _{1 \leq i \leq m}\mathbb {P}(f(Y^n) \neq i \mid W = i) - worst case over all transmitted messages prob of error.
P_{e} = \frac {1}{M}\sum _{i = 1}^{M} \mathbb {P}(f(Y^n) \neq i \mid W = i) - average prob of error.

We have the following components
\mathcal {X} - input alphabet |\mathcal {X}| < \infty
\mathcal {Y} - output alphabet |\mathcal {Y}| < \infty
P_{Y|X} - transition prob matrix

A rate R \geq 0 is achievable if
\lim _{n\to \infty } P_{e, \text {max}} = 0
\lim _{n \to \infty } \frac {\log _{2}M_n}{n} = R
The channel capacity is the maximum achievable rate i.e. C = \sup \{R \geq 0 \mid R \text { is achievable}\}

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
We make the following simplifying assumptions (for now)
We assume no feedback. This means that if we have W \to X_{1} \to Y_{1} \to X_{2} \to \cdots then X_i does not use the knowledge of previous channel outputs i.e. Y^{i - 1} \leftrightarrow (W, X^{i - 1}) \leftrightarrow X_i.
Random encoding: X^n(i) is drawn i.i.d from some specified distribution P_X.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The information channel capacity of a DMC (\mathcal {X}, \mathcal {Y}, P_{Y|X}) is defined as C(P_{Y|X}) = \max _{P_X}I(X; Y)
Non rigorous: says that channel capacity and information channel capacity are equal.

A channel is symmetric if
each row is a permutation of the first row
each column is a permutation of the first column
It is weakly symmetric if it satisfies 1 but the columns only sum to the same value.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The capacity of a symmetric or weakly symmetric channel is \log (|\mathcal {Y}|) - H(r), where r is the first row of P_{Y|X}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We have three components to the channel coding problem:

html:ol.html:li: Channel encoder
Noisy channel
Channel decoder

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We first pass W to the channel encoder get some X^n which goes into noisy channel and becomes Y^n. Then the goal is for the channel decoder to recover W from Y^n. This means that the goal of the encoder is to reduce redundancy while also ensuring that there is no overlap when passed through the noisy channel.

A channel (with memory) is a sequence of conditional distributions:_
P_{Y_{1}|X_{1}},P_{Y_{2}|Y_{1},X^{2}},P_{Y_{3}|Y^{2},X^{3}},\ldots ,P_{Y_{n}|Y^{n-1},X^{n}},
where P_{Y_{n}|Y^{n-1},X^{n}} is a collection of distributions on \mathcal {Y} one for every (y^{n-1},x^{n})\in \mathcal {Y}^{n-1}\times \mathcal {X}^{n} .

An encoder (with feedback) with parameter (n,\Theta ) for a channel is a vector of mappings e_{i} for i=1,2,\ldots ,n , where
e_{i}:\{1,2,\ldots ,\Theta \}\times \mathcal {Y}^{i-1}\to \mathcal {X}.

html:ul.html:li: The encoder is a deterministic function of \mathbf {W}
The channel does not have access to the message \mathbf {W}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
The differential entropy h(X) of a continuous random variable X with density f(x) is defined as
h(X)=-\int _{S}f(x)\log f(x)\,dx,
where S is the support set of the random variable.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Information Theory › AEP for Continuous Random Variables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Information Theory › AEP for Continuous Random Variables

fr:title.text: Typical Set for Continuous Random Variables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Typical Set for Continuous Random Variables

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
For \epsilon >0 and any n , we define the typical set A_{\epsilon }^{(n)} with respect to f(x) as follows:
A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}=\left \{(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})\in S^{n}:\left |-\frac {1}{n}\log f(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})-h(X)\right |\leq \epsilon \right \},
where f(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})=\prod _{i=1}^{n}f(x_{i}) .
The volume Vol( A ) of a set A\subset \mathcal {R}^{n} is defined as \text {Vol}(A)=\int _{A}dx_{1}\,dx_{2}\,\cdots \,dx_{n}.

The typical set A_{\epsilon }^{(n)} has the following properties:
\Pr \left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )>1-\epsilon for n sufficiently large.
\mathrm {Vol}\left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )\leq 2^{n(h(X)+\epsilon )} for all n .
\mathrm {Vol}\left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )\geq (1-\epsilon )2^{n(h(X)-\epsilon )} for n sufficiently large.

For, p_{i}=\int _{i\,\Delta }^{(i+1)\,\Delta }f(x)\,d x=f(x_{i})\Delta \,. We define the discrete approximation of the differential entropy as \begin {align*} H(X^{\Delta })&\coloneqq -\sum _{-\infty }^{\infty }p_{i}\log p_{i} \\ &=-\sum _{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x_{i})\Delta \log (f(x_{i})\Delta ) \\ &=-\sum \Delta f(x_{i})\log f(x_{i})-\sum f(x_{i})\Delta \log \Delta \\ &=-\sum \Delta f(x_{i})\log f(x_{i})-\log \Delta , .\end {align*}

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Taking \Delta = 2^{-n}, then X^{\Delta } is an "n-bit" approximation of X.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X be any real valued random variable, and let X^{(1/n)} denote the discretization of X with parameter \Delta =1/n . Then, the information dimension associated with X is defined as
d=\lim _{n\to \infty }\frac {H(X^{(1/n)})}{\log n},
If f: \mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R} is twice-differentiable, then f is convex if and only if \nabla ^2 f(\boldsymbol {x}) \succeq 0.
The following operations preserve convexity

html:ul.html:li: Nonnegative weighted sums: if f_1, \ldots , f_n are convex and \alpha _1, \ldots , \alpha _n > 0 then we have f(\boldsymbol {x}) = \sum _{i = 1}^{n} \alpha _i f_i(\boldsymbol {x}) is convex
Pointwise maximum: if f_1, \ldots , f_n are convex then f(\boldsymbol {x}) = \max _i \{f_i(\boldsymbol {x})\} is convex

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A function f is \mu -strongly convex if and only if g(\boldsymbol {x}) = f(\boldsymbol {x}) - \frac {\mu }{2} ||\boldsymbol {x}||_2^2 is convex, \forall \boldsymbol {x} \in \mathrm {dom} f.
A problem is smooth if both the objective function and the constraints are differentiable.

Let f: \mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R} be convex. A subgradient of f at \boldsymbol {x}_0 is any \boldsymbol {u} satisfying f(\boldsymbol {x}) \geq f(\boldsymbol {x}_0) + \langle \boldsymbol {u}, \boldsymbol {x} - \boldsymbol {x}_0 \rangle

The subdifferential is the set of all subgradient's of f at \boldsymbol {x}_0. \partial f(\boldsymbol {x}_0) \coloneqq \{\boldsymbol {u} | f(\boldsymbol {x}) \geq f(\boldsymbol {x}_0) + \langle \boldsymbol {u}, \boldsymbol {x} - \boldsymbol {x}_0 \rangle \}
In this section, we cover many random math facts / tools that come up / are useful in later optimization problems.

A symmetric matrix \mathbf {A}\in \mathbb {R}^{n\times n} is positive definite if \mathbf {x}^{\top }\mathbf {A}\mathbf {x}>0 for all nonzero \mathbf {x}\in \mathbb {R}^{n}. It is positive semidefinite (p.s.d.) if \mathbf {x}^{\top }\mathbf {A}\mathbf {x}\geq 0 for all \mathbf {x}\in \mathbb {R}^{n}.

Let \mathbf {A}\in \mathbb {R}^{m\times n} with \operatorname {rank}(\mathbf {A})=r. There exist \sigma _{1}\geq \sigma _{2}\geq \cdots \geq \sigma _{r}>0, and matrices \mathbf {U}\in \mathbb {R}^{m\times r},\ \mathbf {V}\in \mathbb {R}^{n\times r}, with orthonormal columns (\mathbf {U}^{\top }\mathbf {U}=\mathbf {I},\ \mathbf {V}^{\top }\mathbf {V}=\mathbf {I}) such that \mathbf {A}\ =\ \mathbf {U}\mathbf {\Sigma }\mathbf {V}^{\top }\ =\ \sum _{i=1}^{r}\sigma _{i}\mathbf {u}_{i}\mathbf {v}_{i}^{\top }\,,\quad \mathbf {\Sigma }\ =\ \operatorname {diag}\left (\sigma _{1},\cdots ,\sigma _{r}\right ).

Let \mathbf {A}\in \mathbb {R}^{m\times n} with SVD, \mathbf {A}=\sum _{i=1}^{\min \{m,n\}}\sigma _{i}\mathbf {u}_{i}\mathbf {v}_{i}^{\top }. Then an optimal solution to the best rank-r approximation problem \min _{\mathbf {X}}||\mathbf {X}-\mathbf {A}||_{2}\,,\quad \text {s.t.}\quad \text {rank}(\mathbf {X})\ \leq \ r is the truncated SVD of the following form \boxed {\widehat {\mathbf {A}}_{r}\ =\ \sum _{i=1}^{r}\sigma _{i}\mathbf {u}_{i}\mathbf {v}_{i}^{\top }}\,,\ \ \text {if}\ \ \sigma _{r}>\sigma _{r+1}\,,\,\text {it is unique.}

Let \mathbf {A}\in \mathbb {R}^{m\times n}. We say the matrix norm is unitary invariant if \left ||\mathbf {A}\right ||_{\sharp }\ =\ \left ||\mathbf {PAQ}\right ||_{\sharp },\quad \forall \ \mathbf {P}\in O(m),\ \mathbf {Q}\in O(n).

Let \mathbf {A}\in \mathbb {R}^{m\times n}. For any p \in [1,+\infty ], the function \left ||\mathbf {A}|\right |_{\mathrm {S}_{p}}\,:=\,\left ||\mathbf {\sigma }(\mathbf {A})|\right |_{p} is a norm on \,\mathbb {R}^{m\times n}.

A differentiable function f(\mathbf {x}) is called smooth iff it has a Lipschitz continuous gradient, i.e., iff L< \infty such that ||\nabla f(\mathbf {x})-\nabla f(\mathbf {z}) ||_{2}\ \leq L ||\mathbf {x}-\mathbf {z} ||_{2},\quad \forall \mathbf {x},\ \mathbf {z}\in \mathbb {R}^{n}

Let f:\mathbb {R}^{n}\mapsto \mathbb {R} be continuously differentiable. For any fixed \mathbf {x} and \mathbf {y}, we have \,f(\mathbf {y})\,=\,f(\mathbf {x})\,+\,\langle \nabla f(\mathbf {z}(t_{L})),\mathbf {y}-\mathbf {x}\rangle \, for some \mathbf {z}(t_{L})=(1-t_{L})\cdot \mathbf {x}+t_{L}\cdot \mathbf {y} with t_{L}\in (0,1)\,.

html:ul.html:li: If f:\mathbb {R}^n\mapsto \mathbb {R} is continuously differentiable, \begin {aligned} |f(\pmb {x})-f(\pmb {y})| &\leq & \sup _{\pmb {z} \in \mathbb {R}^n} ||\nabla f(\pmb {z})||_2 \, ||\pmb {x}-\pmb {y}||_2 \end {aligned} so that the Lipschitz constant of f is \sup _{\pmb {z} \in \mathbb {R}^n} ||\nabla f(\pmb {z})||_2.

If f:\mathbb {R}^{n}\mapsto \mathbb {R} is twice continuously differentiable, ||
abla f( oldsymbol {x})-
abla f( oldsymbol {y})||_{2}\ \leq \ \sup _{ oldsymbol {z}\in \mathbb {R}^{n}}||
abla ^{2}f( oldsymbol {z})||_{2}\,|| oldsymbol {x}- oldsymbol {y}||_{2} so that the Lipschitz constant of 
abla f is \sup _{ oldsymbol {z}\in \mathbb {R}^{n}}||
abla ^{2}f( oldsymbol {z})||_{2}

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f:\mathbb {R}^{n}\to \mathbb {R} be continuously differentiable, and \nabla f(\mathbf {x}) is \gamma _{L}(\mathbf {x}_{0})-Lipschitz continuous at \mathbf {x}_{0}, then \left |f(\mathbf {x})-\widehat {f_{L}}(\mathbf {x};\mathbf {x}_{0})\right |\ \leq \ \frac {\gamma _{L}(\mathbf {x}_{0})}{2}\,||\mathbf {x}-\mathbf {x}_{0}||_{2}^{2}\,, where we define \widehat {f_{L}}(\mathbf {x};\mathbf {x}_{0})\ :=\ f(\mathbf {x}_{0})\ +\ \langle \nabla f(\mathbf {x}_{0}),\mathbf {x}-\mathbf {x}_{0}\rangle \,.
